comments.commentBody:
I'm excited to make this in the fullness of summer! I have used her cookbook, "The Gift of Southern Cooking" for many years and recommend it. (Perhaps there's a copy of it or her other books in your local library.)
Her story of growing up in an all African-Amerucan village in Freetown,Virginia is full of insight into the food culture of people who knew how to relish everything they grew, collected, hunted, or fished. Later on as an influential chef in NYC, she became one of the 
first to influence the farm to table movement
that emphasizes seasonal provisions.
I’m going to make this with my Italian fancy canned peaches. I will use the syrup in the gorgeous glass jar, add brandy, and take it from there. What a lovely way to upgrade canned peaches in syrup.

@Janice typically when you heat the peaches un a simmering liquid the 
skin slips right off....remove the skin.

Can you provide the recipe using 7 lbs of peaches?  I would like to make this and can it using water bath canning.  Or should I just scale up the recipe based on the weight of peaches I am using?  I ask because often with jams and jellies scaling up doesn't work well.

For those who want to can the peaches in this recipe — because there are no canning instructions included here, I would not try and can them.

I do not think there is enough sugar included in the recipe.  Keep in mind the sugar is the preservative here.  

I recommend you go to state extensions to find a recipe for canning.
Would love to try this, but am confused—is the skin left on the peaches after rubbing off the fuzz, or is all the skin rubbed off? Or, does the skin simply fall off as peaches summer?
The skin is kept on for the peach to stay intact.   Usually for a pie you blanch the peaches to easily remove just the skin,  unlike peeling which is a pain because peaches are ultra slippery.
I know this sounds silly, but can you substitute something for the brandy
@Margaret Slomin I made this with bourbon instead of brandy and my guests all loved it.
Don't understand why brandy isn't added to the initial cooking of the peaches.

Could this be processed for canning to make them shelf-stable do we think?  Would love to know anyone's thoughts on this, I'm just a beginner canner, but I'm really eager to expand my repertoire beyond jams and jellies!

Why does this call for bottles/purified water?  Given we are boiling anyway, rinsing with tap water, and serving immediately rather than canning I couldn’t think of why we would need more than tap water throughout the recipe.
Serves 8-12???  My best friend made this with about 4 peaches, halved.  It was super delicious and it served 4!   One peach per person didn’t seem excessive!

I did it the usual way—blend dry and wet ingredients together in separate bowls, mix together in three stages using a wooden spoon.  Then add melted and cooled butter last. And yes, reduce the sugar to one cup as per today’s preference.  And then, i mix the dry ingredients for one extra cake since I have the ingredients out on the counter anyway, and put it in a canning jar in the cupboard, so this cake is already half made for the next time.
@Earlene You did t beat the butter and sugar together? Only added the butter at the end?

I've made this at least 50 times over the years.  Don't forget the vanilla and nutmeg!  Sometimes I lightly sprinkle turbinado or demerara sugar on the top before baking.  The cake flavor goes nicely with blueberry compote also.

My mother always made a version of this "plain cake," and it was about the only cake we had, a favorite of us all. She served it with a hot sauce, made with powdered sugar, butter, vanilla, and water. The old Betty Crocker cookbooks called it "dinette cake" or "one-egg cake."
Dinette Cake was made by my Mother, from the 1956 Betty Crocker cookbook, I believe.

Just seeing Edna Lewis’ name makes me happy.  I’ve been making this cake for 30 years and shared the recipe often with friends.  Cream of Tartar is a kitchen staple for this recipe and Lewis’ famous biscuits.

Please note that most commercially available double-acting baking powder does NOT have cream of tartar.  In the 1970's (because of cost?) cream of tartar was removed from single-acting baking powder and double-acting baking powder remained.  This topic was covered in the NY "Times" in a "De gustibus" column by Craig Claiborne published Jan. 5, 1976

I just made this cake a few hours ago, I like the crumb a lot. It might seem like a lot of sugar but it is just fine. I think I put a bit too much nutmeg and the aroma is a bit overwhelming but pairing it with coffee or tea would help to dissuade the aroma. Overall is not bad but I guess that for the time and amount of ingredients is perfect if you, quite literally, don't have much time and don't wanna use many utensils and machinery.

@Oscar C
Edna Lewis helped teach me to cook. This very simple cake is probably meant to have an accompaniment such as fresh fruits or ice cream. It would be sweet but fabulous with a drizzle of caramel frosting.
I’m sure that Mrs. Kelly’s unfrosted cake from the Betsy-Tacy books is a version of this cake.
@Hehst a great memory!

My mother made this busy day cake for decades. She would let it cool, slice it in half horizontally, put layer of jam or jelly on the bottom half and replace the top half over the jelly, then generously dust the cake with powdered sugar.  Delicious!
For a very simila but smaller cake for a singleton or couple, use the old Fannie Merrit Farmer one egg cake.

It sunk in middle before removing from oven - big disappointment with cost of ingredients today and for the time it took to prepare it. 375 is ridiculously high temperature - cske was dark crust in 40 min. My Aunt Sarah’s cake recipe is no fail - I will revert back to her recipe.
"The cake has a beautiful rise but may sink a bit in the middle; that’s just part of its humble charm." Did you miss this?

@P colquhoun
Update: I iced the sunken cake with home made butter icing and it was delicious ! The crumb was perfect! Best white cake ever!

Also, regarding the note about double-acting baking powder, it may be that the sensitivity to the aluminum taste is genetic, similar to how some people think cilantro tastes bad. I wondered for years why some baked goods left a bad taste in my mouth and finally realized it was because of the baking powder. For my own baking, I’ve switched to an aluminum-free version and am much happier!

Homemade baking powered was a flop! I mixed up homemade baking powder according to directions and used the amount called for - my cake exploded and poured all down the sides of the pan while baking. Bummer waste of ingredients!
I used the homemade baking powder too and my cake exploded. Yes, it was a total waste of ingredients. About 1/4 of my cake was done and the rest is still wet. What I tasted was nothing special.

Simple, yet delicious! Yes, it sunk in the middle :( 
With a little research (Shirley Corriher's "BakeWise") I discovered the reason-too much baking powder. 
I made it again, reducing the baking powder to 2 1/2 teaspoons and like magic, the center rose to a gentle dome and remained aloft!

Made this for the first time tonight. Easy to make and came out wonderfully. 

I bumped up the nutmeg.  And only used 1 1/3c sugar (what Edna Lewis specifies in the book recipe), and that was a good call. Doesn’t need to be any sweeter. 

Served with a small scoop of mango ice cream.

On the tip about making your own baking powder, that Claiborne column from 1976 has a different recipe: two parts cream of tartar, one part baking soda, one part cornstarch. Joy has a different recipe: two parts cream of tartar, one part baking soda, half part salt.

Made as written although my oven required 8 extra minutes after testing at 40 minutes (and my oven thermometer shows correct temp). Sprinkled demerara sugar on top before baking. Delicious cake—perfect in the afternoon with coffee.
An error on my part—baked at 350 degrees instead of 375…
This is a lovely plain cake. It can be dressed up for company with ice cream and maybe a little hot fudge. It was fun to make with just a wooden spoon and no electric utensils.

As someone who was raised in the South, I had to smile at the thought of baking a cake while the vegetables cook at the other end of the wood stove. Most of the vegetables I ate as a child were either raw or complete mush, and the idea of cooking vegetables on the stove for 40 minutes is anathema to our more modern way of eating. Well, except for summer collard greens. I could see cooking those for 40 minutes! God bless Ms. Lewis; she was a treasure.

One of the best recipes in the NYT cookbook--astonishingly good. Made as described with strawberry rhubarb compote on the side. Would be wonderful drizzled with rosemary or lemon syrup, topped with ice creame or whipped cream and fruit; or plain and simple for breakfast or with tea. Can't wait to try it split, filled with thinned marmalade and served with a bit of cream poured over it.

This was just delightful. I followed the recipe exactly, and found the subtle flavors and delicate crumb unbeatable. And it lasted 5 days in a ziploc. Tomorrow I'll try substituting cardamom and fior di sicile for the nutmeg and vanilla, and serving it with the first sweet strawberries of the year.

It seemed like an awfully lot of batter for an 8 inch square pan. Plus, it has four whole teaspoons of baking powder and three eggs. That's a lot of leavening! I thought about putitng it in a 9 by 13 pan, but decided to follow the recipe. My fire alarm ended going off and I have a bunch of burnt batter in my oven. The cake is half done. The top browned and the batter is still wet. This is by far the most disastrous cake baking experience I have ever had.
What am I missing? Commenters keep referring to a one egg cake. Thus one has three… and what honestly seems like a lot of butter for an 8x8. Sorry.
does anyone have any suggestions for how to scale this for a 9*13 in pan? it sounds like a good recipe for a crowd.

@Catherine Multiply the dimensions of the pan called for in the recipe (8x8=64) and the pan you want to scale up (or down) to (9x13=117). That gets you the area in square inches of each pan. Then divide the area of your pan by the area of the recipe pan: 117/64=1.828. That tells you that you’ll need to scale up the recipe by 1.8. I would probably just double it for ease of calculating amounts and then leave out a little of the batter when filling the pan if it seems like it would be too much.

You could probably just double the ingredients because the 8x8 is 64 sq inches, and double that is 128 sq in. A 9x13 is 117 sq inches, so your cake will be just less than 10% too big. But it's tough to adapt recipes with eggs because "half an egg" isn't practical.

I am trying to find a recipe for old fashioned Rea Cake we had in Mississippi as a child.  It had a simple UNwritten recipe that some older people had ingredients similar to the ingredients listed here. It was heavy and crumbly and not too sweet, baked in a square pan in the oven.  I often wondered it it didn't also have some white cornmeal mixed in.   Anyone familiar with this tea cake?  I have found recipes for cookies called Tea Cakes, but not for an actual cake.
@Jessica LeCroy.  It should read TEA cake
@Jessica LeCroy does this look like your tea cake? https://southernbite.com/old-fashioned-skillet-cake-or-plain-cake/
And she wasn't wrong, commercial double acting baking sodas still have a bitter taste in my in my opinion.
What is the downside to not beating by hand?

I loved this cake! Very nice sponge and flavor. It does sink a bit, but it doesn’t bother me. I made a fresh blueberry compote and added a big spoonful over the top with a small dollop of whipped cream cheese. Delicious! It’s an easy cake to make. Although I realized in the beginning of baking that I didn’t have nutmeg, so just used cinnamon.
Can you add a cup of chopped rhubarb to it?
The sugar and butter can be blended in a kitchen aid mixer.
I want to make this cake but isn’t 4 teaspoons of baking powder too much?

I used one third of the sugar and one third of the butter. Reduced the baking powder a bit too and made it in a loaf pan at 350. Loved the flavor.  Brought many childhood memories. Thank you for the recipe.
This is a recipe that could greatly benefit by adding a video showing how to.
@Dagwood Search for the TikTok Doner kebab video by MezeMike mentioned in head note. Very cool.
Broil the meat after you’ve unraveled it to get it extra crispy! I also tried this recipe with ground turkey and it worked great! Ate it on a bed of cucumber yogurt sauce

@Birdie
Details, please
You've got to take off the top sheet after rolling. That direction IS NOT in the recipe. This is a critical error in the written method.
@Donald I’ve made it a few times without removing that top sheet of parchment and everything worked out fine. It was delicious.

@Donald oh no! I’ve just about finished cooking mine and did not do that!
Wish me luck!
@Donald Thanks, I was wondering about that.
I love that  Step 3 specifies “using clean hands”

The recipe sounds delicious, and the trick with the parchment paper is interesting.  But if I just wanted the flavor of Doner kebab, why couldn't I just get out my griddle and make thin smashburgers, slice them up and stuff some pitas, along with the usual accompaniments?

@Bill Respectfully, bill, no one is saying you can’t, but then you’d have a smash burger in a pita, which is not the same as a doner kebab.  They’re similar in some ways, but not the same.  It be that way with recipes.

@George Kennedy Respectfully, this recipe uses a sheet pan and parchment, not the traditional döner kebab preparation…so much for authentic. It seems Bill’s ultra-thin smash burger idea might achieve a similar meaty-crispy result and could be just as valid an alternative method. Let us know how it goes, Bill!👍🏻
@Bill why not? Sounds great and easier
I step from the referenced video (MezeMike) that was omitted from this recipe: after unrolling the meat from the parchment, it was put back into the oven to crisp up.

I did a half recipe on a 1/4 sheet pan in my countertop Breville oven. Baked at 350F convection. Worked like a charm. Also, I'm a cake decorator and I found using my offset spatula to spread the meat was neat, fast and simple. Much better than using your hands.This is a great recipe.
Do you then remove the top parchment paper before rolling?  The MezeMike video does not include that top parchment.
Yes, Michael, you do! That it isn't written into the instructions is a major mistake.
@Donald You definitely don’t have to? I’m confused, are you guys not using actual parchment paper? With parchment paper, it should work fine
@Donald it works with the double layer. I'll try without next time
I found I had a lot of reserved drippings.  I'd recommend putting the yogurt in a separate bowl and adding just enough of the drippings to get the taste and consistency you like.
@Tanya agree. I would quadruple the yogurt sauce too. I wanted much more. So tarry

This sauce from later posts is to die for – 4 tbsp Greek yogurt, 4 minced garlic cloves, 1 tsp dried parsley, 1 tsp pepper, 1 tsp kosher salt, plus scant ¼ cup reserved cooking juices. I made a version half lamb and half beef which was terrific.

Very tasty! I followed the recipe exactly and I used half beef(90), half lamb. I roasted quartered tomatoes & red onion along side the rolls and made a separate red onion, sumac, parsley and lemon juice mix for an extra topping. One thing to note in case it's not clear- after flattening the meat mixture, remove the top parchment and roll using just the bottom sheet. Great recipe!
Have made this 5 times since Mezemike’s video - it’s so good!1

Made this tonight (yes on 12/23).  We love to make different foods for holidays instead of the usual stuff.  This was just delightful.  The process is really brilliant, you get the thin slices of lamb and it has crispy bits.  Thank you for this idea.

This recipe caught my eye, but I don't want meat. I froze  a block of tofu, thawed it, and then used the slicer on my box grater to shave it . I followed the rest of the steps exactly. It was delicious and is going into the meal rotation.
A vegetarian version of this is also wonderful. Instead of lamb, use falafel patties and fill the pita pocket with lettuce, onions, cilantro, tomatoes, etc. Top off with hummus and lime juice.
@Srini excellent suggestion if you want falafels instead of what this recipe produces

When making the rolls, keep them as round as possible. If you flatten the rolls, the meat will be thicker at the edges. I make these regularly with various meats and they are great. Especially great with a simple homemade Turkish pide bread. Quick and easy to make. I usually crisp the meat in a hot cast iron with butter before serving.

This one was really good and not hard, I made some tweaks as other commenters suggested. I removed the second parchment before rolling, I broiled it briefly before serving for crispiness, I also tossed the meat with lemon and some reserved drippings for more authentic greasiness. Lastly, I made a tzatziki sauce instead of just plain yogurt. I'm just a total tzatziki stan, and I compromised by replacing the oil in it with the fat from the pan.

This was so good! I used ground turkey because I don’t eat beef or lamb. I wish I would have known about popping the sheet pan into the oven after removing the parchment paper to get the meat crispier. Other than that, delicious!

Delicious! Definitely takes more than 5 minutes of prep but worth the effort. 

As someone who loves spice, I found a full tablespoon of red pepper to be a bit much. Would recommend to use less if subbing for the Aleppo. 

I read the comments too late and threw it in the oven without removing the top sheets of parchment paper. It came out just fine.

is there a non dairy substitue for the yogurt? 
don't eat milk and meat together

@Levi
Plant based yogurt or tofu mashed and puréed  with water or plant milk
@LFB in Cyprus this is always served with tahini sauce in the pita.  No dairy involved

@Levi I mixed mayonnaise in with the meat instead of yogurt and it turned out tasty. I haven’t tried it with the yogurt so I’m not sure how it compares exactly, but it definitely still tasted delicious!

You do get crispy bits, especially if you put the seam of the rolled parchment down on the sheet.  Some parts are softer than others, but nothing was too soft.  The one thing I’d recommend here is to line the sheet pan with foil.  Otherwise it’s a mess to clean up.  It was super-tasty!  I froze half the meat mixture instead of making four rolls, and I’m looking forward to cooking it again.

@Siobhan and @ cocomero,
 I just posted a comment on how I made it with tofu. I'm a vegetarian but my husband isn't. He had seconds and said he didn't think anyone would guess that it was tofu .  I freeze, thaw, and press my tofu before using. I shaved it thin with a box grater and then followed the rest of the steps.

I’ve made these with the parchment and they are delicious!!!! I’ll admit that working with the parchment was awkward, and I did tell my husband not to fall in love with them as they were not that fun to make and I’d no intention of ever making again, but honestly, they are so darn good I’ll do it all over again. The family loved them!

I made half a batch using lamb only.  I still did two roll ups which was, I think, a mistake because they completely fell apart when unwrapped.  My initial bake was 30 minutes, followed by 2 minutes under the broiler.  The broiling added a nice texture and flavor.

When I tried to drain the juices halfway through, the rolls shifted and I could not completely drain the tray.  At the end, there was this burned sludge left where the juices had been.  I finished it on a fresh sheet.

I think for 1/2 batch I should have done one roll.  I think I had it too thin because it fell apart so much.  Perhaps a heavier roll would not have shifted as much during drainage.  I used purchased tzatziki so I did not use the reserved juices.

Also, I only used 1/2 or a huge red onion which was plenty.  Discretion on the onion…I think that the juicy onions may have contributed to the “sludge”.

It was still good enough to be requested again!

I used premium mixed pepper flakes and it was a bit spicier than expected!  So be careful if you don’t want it too spicy.

January 2026 I’m seeing this recipe and I copied it but I’m a very visual person and I’m really having a hard time visualizing this parchment paper process. It would be so helpful to have some pictures to accompany it for us visual learners… It sounds so good, but there’s a disconnect in my understanding… I guess I’ll read through some of the posts to see if I can get a better sense of things… Thank you for your consideration
@MarkO check out the TikTok video referenced in the head note

ok, ok, I have yet to make this recipe (I will tonight) but all the people recommending the TikTok video get my vote just because of the accent!! She should bottle that and sell it! https://www.tiktok.com/@becs_journey_to_health/video/7579991249264889110
@Yasmeen indeed her accent is amazing!  Loved the video.
This was a win/win and we can't WAIT to have it again. The only change I made was reducing the crushed red pepper (to ½ tsp) because we're wimps, but I'd give this 10 stars if I could!

Great recipe and not difficult. However, would someone specify/suggest how much of the pan drippings to mix with the 1/2 cup of yogurt, or a ratio?  I had a lot of drippings so mixed with the yogurt yielded a very watery sauce. I dumped out 1/2 of my mix and added more yogurt, twice, still with a very liquidly sauce but diluted flavor. I know I could use corn starch but, would rather get it right the first time.
I probably had one part drippings to two parts yoghurt and it was pretty much spot on.
All the videos online instruct to remove the top piece of parchment. Is this an error in NYT recipe?

This recipe is the closest I’ve found to the döner kebabs I had in Freiburg, Germany. It’s not exactly the same, but it comes pretty close. Also, they used Fladenbrot instead of pita. It’s a Turkish bread with a light, fluffy texture and a soft, slightly chewy finish. If you can’t find Fladenbrot where you live, it’s actually pretty easy to make at home.

Very good. Made it with ground turkey and it came out delicious. Definitely recommend broiling the meat after unrolling it to give a little burnish and crisp. Made a white sauce of 1/2 cup mayo, 1/2 cup greek yogurt, and about a tablespoon of tahini (it was all that was left in the jar), microplaned one large clove of garlic, drizzled in some olive oil and squeeze of lemon juice. Served with Turkish red cabbage and other veggies to wrap.

I made this easily in my small French kitchen with my small oven - it is significantly faster and easier than one would expect reading the instructions.  Used half lamb and half beef (and halved the recipe - I made 2 rolls).  Will definitely make again in my larger US kitchen.  I got a lot of liquid and made a sauce by adding about 1/4 cup to 1/4 c yoghurt.  I will skim the fat and continue to make my sauce "on demand" as I eat the leftovers.  (Served on a bed of roasted potato wedges.)

Used ground chicken.  I wish onion measurements were in cups - I think I had too many onions, and my mix was on the loose side.  I stuck it in the fridge uncovered, about 20 mins to tighten it up a bit.  To make sure the spices were mixed in thoroughly, I put the spices and the yogurt in the food processor after making the onion paste, and then mixed the whole mixture into the meat.  Broiled at the end as suggested by other comments. Really good and would make again.
I’ve made this once (using a slightly different recipe)  with Impossible meat. It was great. This is one viral recipe I can get behind.
Really delicious.  The sauce with the drippings is divine.  I would suggest to brown up the meat under a broiler after it's been unrolled.  Put the undercooked parts facing upward.
Ran out of parchment paper after a few rolls and made the rest into meatballs. It was awesome.

One tiny simplification: You don't need a rolling pin and multiple sheets of parchment. I used an offset spatula to spread the meat in a thin layer on a piece of parchment and rolled it up. So simple. 

This recipe is a trip to Greece without an airplane ticket!

If I didn't live with a Turkish person, I would give it 5 stars. However, he nearly died laughing that I was calling this döner. It was tasty and I'll make it again. But be warned: Don't present this to a Turk as döner.
there are some really good recipes for pita, and they're quite easy.
Made exactly as directed. Perfect and just like the delicious donner kebab I've had from stands, trucks and cafes. Thank you for providing an at-home method.  About to make a second batch.

I thought this was a great recipe. 
I didn’t removed the top sheet of parchment as others have suggested and it worked just fine. My parchment didn’t turn dark brown even after cooking it a little longer but the meat had crispy edges and was delicious.
The only think I did differently was to remove a fair bit of the fat from the pan drippings before adding it to the yogurt - oh and I added chopped mint!
Served a Greek salad in the side with feta - splendid!
made this with impossible lite ground "beef" and it was delicious.

I was very impressed with how delicious the lamb turned out. Perhaps a bit heavy on the red pepper flakes, I felt it dominated the other seasonings. I added chopped fresh parsley to the meat mixture. I removed the top sheet of parchment before rolling and baking and also broiled it on high for 4 minutes after unrolling the meat onto the sheet. The broiling is essential for crispy goodness. The sauce couldn't have been tastier or easier to make. I'd love to make this for my Cypriot friend!

Quite good! Cut the salt in the meat by 1/3 and it was still plenty salty and used fresh garlic in with the onion. Very solid result though and will keep this on hand as a satisfying dinner with good leftovers for lunches.

The family agreed this is a keeper!! The meat was soft when I took it out; a few minutes under the broiler got some delicious crispy bits. I used cottage cheese instead of yogurt with the meat (all I had) and served with tahini sauce, chopped tomatoes and pickles. I only had 96% lean meat so added another tablespoon of olive oil
This was so easy and super yummy!  I've also done it with ground turkey and that turned out great!  I broiled it at the end to give it a little more browning.  Make sure it is rolled then.

I found this to be strangely under seasoned. I was surprised how bland the meat was and the drippings were also lacking flavor. I will probably try again, but will definitely up the spices and salt.

It was also very soft and wet when I unrolled it after 45 minutes. I went ahead and gave it an additional 15 minutes at 500° convection which helped, but I can’t help but think it has to do with the highly varying quantity of “2 medium onions” in the ingredients. Did I have too much? Were they excessively wet? I don’t know what the recipe writer considers a medium onion (in my area, we only seem to get one size of onion), so the recipe would benefit from specifying approximate amounts in cups or grams.

Super fun to make. Store bought pitas were disappointing. Good store bought Naan was better and easier for me to find than good store bought pitas. The yogurt mixed with drippings was excellent. I also made mint taziki. Served with lettuce and tomatoes
This was so much easier than anticipated and my family absolutely loved it. Will make again for sure.

I made this for dinner tonight. I used half lamb and half beef. I blended the meat in the food processor to make a paste. Used parsley, cumin, coriander, turmeric, oregano, marjoram, sumac, and salt and pepper.
This parchment paper method is genius.

5-7 minutes under the broiler after unfurling really helps crisp up the bits. Same for reheating.

Works great with ground turkey too!
this tasted delicious. after removing the paper I put the parts that weren't browned back in the oven for 5 mins. It would also be better with tzatiki vs the yogurt dripping sauce.

Made for the first time, but halved it. Otherwise, tried to keep recipe as recommended. Overall, easy and delicious. I love the onion oil paste! I forgot to half the olive oil but figured I could make worse mistakes, - and it came out crispy and tasty. I made fresh pita bread for the first time too; had time because the doner meat was so easy. I’d reduce the amount of hot pepper (I guess that’s another sub because I didn’t want to buy aleppo pepper and am thinking now that’s not as hot), and my yogurt sauce was kind of thin, but probably my errors. Everything was still super tasty and a great way to enjoy lamb.

Kids and husband all loved this one. PSA: if you forget the yogurt in the meat mixture just keep going as I did; it turned out fine. I cannot believe the texture of the meat that came out—really nice  to pile on a pita. Husband couldn’t believe it started as ground beef. Thanks for the mom out there who shared this with the world. Served with a simple Greek salad. Best part: there are leftovers even though I have two teen boys. Stayed on theme and did the chocolate crunch cake for dessert. Try both!!!
Great method! Absolutely delicious, but we will cut down on the onion when making again. 1 seems plenty.

Very good, will make again. Subs made based on pantry availability. Used sweet paprika, beef, Morton salt would start with 1/2 amount of recipe. Served with Whole Foods 365 pita. Awful- gummy and fell apart. The fresh ground pepper was great in this.

Incredible. These are so good, an excellent kick, and who doesn't love a warm pita? The process was new to me, but the results speak for themselves. Next time, I will get some butter lettuce instead, maybe some cucumbers too.
Why 85- to 90-percent ground beef? Isn’t lamb closer to 20%?
Is this yummy ?

@Andy

You’re going to have to try it yourself and then decide!

"his TikTok, @MezeMike, " 
Having the same thought as Dagwood)- I went to the article:  in the text, they have a link to his TikTok page, where you can indeed watch him make it.
As a Berliner I know that if you want to taste the original Döner you don’t have to travel to the Middle East but to Berlin. Specifically to the  Kreuzberg neighborhood.
@Real Berlineri’ve never been able to find a replica of the Doner I had in Freiburg’s food hall.
The drippings end up making the sauce much too watery. An amount or a warning is necessary.

This was fantastic. Only thing I did differently was I pulsed everything in the food processor briefly after making the onion paste.  So good, and is great the next day too.  I used 50/50 beef & lamb.
This is great. Super easy and the family loved it.   Can’t wait to make it again and let the meat marinade overnight

This recipe is great and it makes a ton of food. We followed the directions to the letter and it was delicious. The sauce was not to my liking as it was a bit greasy. We served it with pickled onions, tzatziki, hummus, tomatoes, lettuce, radishes, and a few classic middle eastern salads. Lucky for us, we have an incredible shop that sells freshly baked pitas so, it was extra nice. 

Because there were a ton of leftovers, we tossed the meat into a huge salad with rice. Excellent!
This makes so much meat! And my 5 year old even liked it.

Made this tonight. Highly adapted. Used ground turkey. Didn’t have onion, so I used a whole head of garlic with the oil as the paste. Used beef shawarma seasoning in place of the other seasonings except I did keep the smoked paprika. I needed longer before draining the drippings because the pan was still dry when the first timer went off. My family loved it. My husband loves gyro and loved this.

Excellent, a fun technique! Foil in the sheet pan make for minimal cleanup. Served with pita, diced onion, beit alpha cucumber, tomato, feta cheese and the yogurt sauce. Next time I’ll use some grated garlic instead of the powder.

This was absolutely delicious! Yes, you can crisp it afterwards; yes, remove the top parchment before rolling (or don’t if the meat is sticking), yes watch the ratio of drippings and yoghurt (and use drippings to bake rice the next day). BUT JUST MAKE IT, you will have fun and you will enjoy every bite!!
We made this for Easter; so good. Easy and impressive meal.

I’ll start off by saying that this recipe was absolutely delicious and went by fast in my house. However, I did have a couple issues:
It took twice as long to make as the recipe says (I started cooking at 530 and dinner was on the table at 745), mainly because the spreading process is so difficult. Having the parchment sheet on top makes it so that- no matter how much meat you put -some of it will inevitably end up spilling out the sides getting all over your counter and rolling pin. In addition, if you put too much or too little meat you have no way of changing the amount without starting all over again. I found that simply using a wet hand- although a bit more tedious -ended up being less messy and gave me more flexibility for the amount of meat in my kebabs. 
I would try this again, but starting much earlier and with the different spreading technique.
Me and my fiance portion the mixture evenly into 4 balls, kind of like hamburgers. Then we spread it between parchment. Maybe that will help.

This was actually amazing. Made 1/2 recipe and broiled to crispiness per comments at conclusion. Accompanied with "copycat recipe" Halal guys white sauce. Served along with chopped salad--tomatoes, red onion, cucumber, feta, kalamata olives, lemon, olive oil, salt, and pepper. Family was clamoring for more.
Anyone find good pita in nyc?
@JBinNYC Can't help with your quest but I can tell you that pita are the easiest breads in the world and once you make them, you'll never buy another.
@JBinNYC: Kalustyan’s and Sahadi.
The triangular pita at Damascus bakery (down the block from Sahadi's on Atlantic Avenue (Brooklyn).

Our family doesn’t eat beef or lamb, so I frequently sub ground chicken or turkey in recipes that call for ground beef. Tempted to try that here. It seems like if rolled thin enough, it would cook to a safe temp. Any thoughts/advice/warnings on this?

@Essbee Tried this with turkey and turned out amazing! I just stuck a thermometer in the middle of the roll after it cooked and it was well over 165. Broiled it to get it crispy after unraveling. There weren’t any drippings so we just made a cucumber yogurt mixture to eat it with.

Ground poultry with a high fat content would likely work well, and is also somewhat authentic. I fell in love with Doner on a visit to Berlin years ago, and one place I went to offered chicken and lamb. A Doner restaurant I visited stateside only offered chicken and beef. So chicken is not unheard of. I personally would not use Turkey because I don't think it works with the Doner spice profile, but you can certainly try it as long as it's not breast meat.
Can this be made without the yogurt?
@Eric I tried this first without yogurt and had no problems. Honestly didn’t differ that much from the version with the yogurt mixed in.
The meat looks dry in the photo. Can that be a problem?
@David DeKok I think for most people who want their meat on the crispy side, it’s a feature, not a bug.

Questions.  Is Doner Kebab of all varieties made with this particular lamb mixture? Could this lamb mixture could be shaped onto a skewer like a sausage and then grilled to make the Doner Kebab that I had and loved back in the day when I visited Turkey?

@Ruth Ann:  Döner was originally made from very thin slices of lamb, alternating with super thin slices of fat from the tail of a fat-tailed sheep. It was rotated vertically (though there is a horizontal version called cağ kebap) in front of a charcoal fire, later gas, then electric. Charcoal is way better! So this is an approximation of döner, not döner, really. Still, it is a very clever technique!

This was very good but the recipe does not explain how to make it. You must cook the onions for at least 15 minutes on low with extra olive oil oil when that is completed add the potatoes cook them 10 minutes or so then add the tomato. Cook them a bit now add the corn water. Cook until the potatoes are soft, but still hold their shape. Add theadd half-and-half and the court.
@Cynthia, I think you’re commenting on the wrong recipe. No potato here.
Or corn water
Birdie your broiling method sounds so much easier and you have experience with it. I’m going to try it first.

I am sure this is tasty, but a Döner kebab it is not. The meat should come sliced from standing rotisserie and the bread for the  Döner kebab "sandwich" is a Turkish flatbread, very much not like a pita (invented in the 1970s by turkish immigrants in Berlin).

@Kate

Well, the whole point here is that most of us don’t have the authentic equipment in our home kitchens!

@Kate Why must people be so darn snarky. The article clearly states it is a “riff”. You either tried it and liked it or you didn’t. Personally, I, along with others appreciate the creative pursuit for a meal I may otherwise not be able to enjoy.
I was skeptical but it turned out delicious. Even my husband from Berlin was enthusiastic!
Is it unnecessary to squeeze the moisture out of the onion? I’ve seen that in other recipes but it’s not in this one
Has anyone tried making this with Impossible or Beyond? My husband is a vegetarian, and I’d love a way to make döner for the family!

@Siobhan

The main part of this technique is about trying to reproduce the texture of the meat. Since it would be difficult to do that with Impossible brand or any meat alternative, I don’t think it would be worth all the work or use of parchment paper. 

I suggest that you use the seasonings and ingredients as a guide to the flavor profile, and just sauté or roast whatever alternative ingredient appeals to you. Now that you brought this up, I think I might try it with tofu crumbles (the smell of Impossible brand products isn’t for me). I’ll reduce the onion amount though, since tofu is a lighter taste than meat. 

If anybody tries doing a veggie version of this, please report back!

@Siobhan
I followed Thee Burger Dude's recipe for vegan Doner Kebab, same method. I used Impossible Beef and it turned out amazing! You can also use Beyond Beef. I tried an internet recipe using TVP and it was just okay.
It tasted like too many taco mixes had been added to the meat.  It was overpowering and nothing like any doner kebab I have ever tasted.

I made with ground chicken and it turned out great (sure lamb would have been even better). The red pepper flakes was a little too much for my son (will cut in half next time)- but the rest of the heat loving house enjoyed it.

This was great, I ended up using one red onion as that's what I had on hand, and then used my stand mixer to fully mix all the ingredients together.  As others called out, I removed the top sheet before rolling!
This technique is amazing and the spices are spot on. We didn't have pita, so served the meat and yogurt sauce with a village salad and home baked bread. Yum!

I made this last night and I am still thinking about it. Definitely one of the best dinner recipes. Not too many dishes and was easy to prepare and make. I used ground turkey instead of lamb and added cayenne instead of paprika for extra spice.
This was so easy and delicious! Definitely watch Meze Mike's video and sneak a few french fries into your finished product.

Made as written and we loved this. Ate on a salad without the pita, no leftovers. Next time I may take the top parchment off for rolling as advised by others to see how it affects the crispness, but the taste made it wonderful even without a super crispy texture.
Watch the MezeMike video. It really helps, I was baffled by the written recipe. This is a wonderful recipe! Just a few tweaks for my personal taste, cut back on the oregano and red pepper flakes.

While I appreciated the method and the thin pieces of kebab, I didn’t love the flavor.  It wasn’t lamb-y enough, or something, to me.  Perhaps it was the yogurt.  I didn’t think a half cup of yogurt for the sauce would be enough (we like sauce) , so I doubled it and added two tablespoons of mayo, in case there weren’t enough drippings.  The drippings were ample, but mostly liquid from the onion and yogurt, rather than lamb fat.  I added a few tablespoons until it was a texture I liked.  We ended up using almost all the sauce, so if you like sauce, I recommend making extra.
loved this recipe. It definitely had the crispy bits that I was looking for and, yes, I did use the parchment. My entire family devoured it!
Delicious!  I would try again.  Mine came out a bit too dry and too much pepper for my liking.

By roll it "like a cinnamon roll", does that mean in a coil shape, because that's how cinnamon rolls are rolled.  If so, how is that done with meat flattened into a pie shape in parchment?  Or does it mean roll it into a cylinder?  Very confusing?
@Michael McDaniel cylinder.  See link to video in Yasmeen post
Cinnamon rolls are made by rolling your big, wide sheet of dough up into a long, fat cylinder, then cutting off the individual rolls. You don’t coil up each roll separately.

I modified mine slightly for the air fryer and it was just so-so. Maybe there was too much yogurt in the meat? It was really wet and soft. Came out super spicy too! Will try again but with some modifications...
Curious if others were able to prep this in 5 minutes as stated in the recipe? Maybe it’s because it was our first time but it took way longer than that…

made mistake of halving recipe for just 2 people...recipe then changed to direct 1/2C filling per sheet.  yielded 4 skinny rolls. shoulve stuck with 1C per, 2 rolls which is what it says you should have at end of rolling a half recipe.  still came out pretty tasty!

This was really good and easy.  Even the very picky child came around to it. I used Turkey and made a yogurt -dill sauce.  It will be a nice option during the summer by using the toaster to cook the meat.
Total winner. 1lb gr lamb easily served 4. Wish I could find good pocket pitas but wrap style was great. Less cooking time required.

These were great!
I made my own sauce as well as a little bit of the "fat yogurt" sauce. I added red cabbage to the tomatoes, onions and lettuce. I'm from Munich and Dürüm (same recipe put in a wrap instead of pita bread) is a stable in my family and they loved these.
Thanks for the recipe.
@Levi try tahini?

Was such a hit last night—my two teenagers had an impromptu sprinting battle to fight for seconds—that we’re making it again tonight. Glad I drained the drippings halfway, as they burned to a crisp by the end. Will drain multiple times tonight. Just flattened onto parchment with my hands (per original video) before rolling and crisped for a bit under the broiler after unfurling.  Will add some feta and cucumber to the pitas tonight.  Love this easy recipe so much!
Really tasty. Like the broil idea. Would do that next time. I would quadruple the yogurt sauce too.   made homemade pita and it all came together so well.

I make this all the time. It comes together quickly and tastes amazing. I'm not a fan of smoked paprika so I leave it out. Tomatoes, cucumbers and yogurt sauce are the perfect additions. I often eat it from a bowl with no pita. Yum.
@Anna frozen tofu is great!

This was a fun project and the meat did in fact end up like shaved lamb.  A few suggestions for cooks in a hurry. Remember to cut all your bottom pieces of paper first so you don’t have to tear them off with goopy hands as you go.  Also you only need to reuse one top sheet - lightly oiled so the mixture does not stick to it.  I found using a rolling pin unnecessary.  I spread the mixture first with a broad silicone spatula and my hands and then did the final spreading with the top sheet and just my hands and it turned out fine.

In terms of ingredients, I followed the recipe exactly, using all lamb and the recommended seasonings. I believed the intensity of the recommended seasonings overwhelmed the delicate lamb flavor. I would definitely use far, far less another time. Maybe use a different meat.

As for draining the fat from a heavy, shallow (foil lined) sheet pant two pounds of meat  rolling around!  Not for me!  I managed to remove it from the oven without spilling the grease all over the place but then I lifted the lamb rolls using two broad spatulas and placed them in a second foil line sheet pan for the last stage.  I let the first pan cool and the fat congeal a bit before I removed it using its foil liner.   I also think disposable foil pans are wonderful for messy situations like this.

title: Wild Greens Salad With Persimmons and Hickory Nuts Recipe
author: Korsha Wilson

comments.commentBody: I'm definitely going to try this- I love persimmons. I'm 48 and live in metro ATL. When I was a kid we just went into a stand of trees in unused lots and picked our own persimmons/plums/berries depending on the season. Where did all of those trees go? I didn't  see persimmons in person again until I moved to the Netherlands (where we foraged for our own hazelnuts growing in a vacant lot:) I'm seriously asking: were they victims of pesticides or some such?

@Atje come to California!  A neighbor just gifted me two giant bags of them because squirrels had nibbled them - I’m more than happy to work around the squirrel bites. But I think the empty lots are gone, sadly.

@Atje

“They paved paradise and put in a parking lot.” Said a very wise woman songwriter decades ago.

Sounds like a perfect salad for Thanksgiving dinner. I love persimmon, especially Fuyu, the firmer more apple-like texture. Wen I lived in Berkeley, this gorgeous fruit tree proliferated everywhere. We had a mature tree in our own backyard and persimmon, was a staple ingredient in the fall. Thanks for the recipe and the memories!
So where do we find hickory nuts?

Delicious! I made this as a lunch salad this December. For Greece, where I live, the recipe is counter-seasonal. Persimmons are now in the markets. Horta, the name that covers at least five different wild greens, are foraged in spring. As suggested by the author, I subbed a bag of grocery store mixed greens. Still five stars!
Those are pecans in the photo of the salad.

Can you use Asian persimmons? I can’t find American ones. Or suggestions on where to buy?

Thanks!!
I live in the midwest and Costco currently has bags of California grown persimmons for sale.
@Deb all persimmons originate in Asia. They were exported to the US in the 1800s. They grow throughout California and in other warmer regions.
This was delicious! A perfect fall salad!

Just want to thank you for featuring foraged greens in a recipe! Here in Northern California, if we have rain in autumn, we are sure to have wild green annuals by the winter solstice - California (miner's) lettuce, chickweed, dock, and mustard cover the fields and hillsides. We are always looking for new ways to serve them. We're putting this on our menu for Xmas eve. Thank you!
Ramps are in season (very briefly) in the early spring. If persimmons are in season in the late fall, how do we make this?

The tamarind concentrate sold in Indian stores is super-strong. (For about 500 ml of homemade ketchup-molasses-vinegar-soy based BBQ sauce, 2 tsp of concentrate (tamarind's used in Worcester sauce) sufficed to give it a brown color and the pleasant sour-sweet tamarind taste. 
I'd start with a quarter jar/6 tbsp and increase to taste in Step 5.
Oxtails are ideal for pressure cooking/instant pot - 1 hour + natural release.
At $13.99 a pound, 7 lbs of oxtails is just shy of $100. That's my grocery budget for the week. This looks delicious and I love oxtails but can't afford to eat them anymore.
I don't know how many people you are cooking for -- but this recipe using 7 pound of oxtails serves 6 to 8 people.  Cut it if you are cooking for fewer!
@Erica I bet you could easily use short fat ribs.
@Erica I was wondering about short ribs. I can’t get oxtails out in the sticks where I live.
Garlic should be halved crosswise not lengthwise.  Lengthwise, I.e., top to bottom only exposes two cloves.

@Josh Maybe the idea was not to have garlic cloves floating in the stew when the oxtails are pulled out? Not sure, but whether halved from top to bottom or side to side, the garlic flavor will definitely be infused in the dish after 3-3.5 hours of braising. Just trying to surmise a reason.
With oxtail both expensive and not always easy to find, might this work with beef short ribs? Seems to me the flavors would go well with another type of rich beef.
The garlic instructions, which are for a head of garlic are missing that each clove in the head should be halved lengthwise.

This recipe really appeals to me, but the directions are a little unclear for me and the ingredients are not very familiar. 

The tip about the pepper is quite helpful, but I’d never heard of guava wine. It doesn’t really seem readily available on the west coast, so I’ll use wine and guava paste. Also, once we cut the head of garlic along its equator (I assume), after it’s cooked do we squeeze out the cloves and mix it into the sauce? Or do we just let those who want the cloves take some? I guess I’m trying to figure out how garlicky this is supposed to taste. 

There also seems a lot of variation on tamarind paste. I really like tamarind flavor, but I don’t have a lot of experience cooking with it. I’m not sure how much to use of the particular jar I happen to have. 

If I make this, I will report back!
How can you adapt this recipe to a pressure cooker?

I would use a pressure cooker instead of a Dutch oven throughout the recipe (it can double as a stovepot.) 
In Step 3,cook at pressure until the first whistle, then turn heat to low, cook for 1 hour intotal, then turn heat off, use natural release (the meat continues to cook as the pressure drops).
Continue from Step 4.
An Instant Pot combines pressure cooking with smarts - you can take a bath, read a book, or leave the house for a short errand: it will stop cooking after the set time has passed
Delicious! I love tamarindo and this is such a luscious way to cook oxtails…
Excited to make this! Wondering, can I substitute date syrup for tamarind paste? If not, I'll have to wait until my market gets it in or until I can get to Kalustean's.
1 full jar of tamarind paste and 1/2 cup of guava paste is wild.

"Strain the cooking liquid into a second large pot and stir in brown sugar, molasses, vinegar and Worcestershire sauce." Does this mean the sugar, molasses, and vinegar go in the strained cooking liquid or a whole separate pot?

Made this last night for a dinner party. It's a lot of work but WORTH IT. Couldn't find Tamarind concentrate but used Tamarind chutney and it worked very well. Almost ruined my brand new Les Cruset because of the sugar burning on the bottom--I blame the guava paste.  It cleaned up well but next time I'd stir it a bit while it's braising. Only had 4 people so used 3.5 lbs oxtails and it was perfect. Adjusted recipe accordingly.
Way too much molasses. We only used half of that 1/4 cup and even that was way too much. I’d say 2TB max. Enjoy the meat flavor not just molasses
Wondering if this can be made a day ahead and gently reheated?
yes, even better.

If I were going to can something for long term storage I would find a recipe specifically written for that purpose. It is important that a recipe has been tested and determined safe for canning. The University of Georgia hosts the National Center for Home Food Preservation. Their site includes numerous recipes. See https://nchfp.uga.edu/how/pickle/relishes-salads/rummage-relish/

This is absolutely fabulous and most delicious!! You can play around a bit with the veggies (I added a bit more green tomatoes) but don’t change anything else. The spices/seeds, sugar and vinegar are in perfect balance. The only suggestion for canning to maintain long-term shelf life is to heat-sterilize the jars in boiling water or in the oven and immediately afterwards fill in the hot chutney. It’s the same exact process as with home-made jam, easy and safe.

@Mary Fran that's the kind I remember. My Grandmother made it. I learned to enjoy it on Pinto beans and cornbread. I wish I had her recipe! But this one sounds delicious and when green tomatoes are in season I will make some! Thank you, NYT, for all the fabulous recipes!!!
Is this chow chow suitable for water-bath canning to make it appropriate for shelf-stable storage?  Would the canning process alter the texture too much?
The chow chow in The South is red, sweet and spicy. Best served with cornbread, blackeyed peas and sweet iced tea.

I lived in the Lower East Side in the 1980s and 1990s and once stumbled into Princess Pamela's restaurant "Princess Pamela's Southern Touch Cuisine"on East Houston with a friend one weeknight.  She looked us over and sat us down.  She yelled over to her cook (can't remember her name - maybe Euphrenia .. something) "what are we cooking tonight?"  Following came honestly one the best meals I have ever eaten. We asked if she had any liquor and she pulled out a jug of wine, and said don't tell.
@Anne P. That place was epic. Nothing like it in the LES these days…
@Anne P. God I miss that era.
Why would you call for 8 tablespoons in a recipe rather than 1/2 cup??
Because people are more likely to fit a tablespoon measure into a jam jar than they are to pour jam out into a liquid measuring cup?
also because the measurement is likely to be more exact.  someone might eyeball 1/2 and it would be actually 3/4. if you use a tablespoon you will probably measure it accurately.
@susan can’t agree.  The total error in 8 measures will likely be greater than one measure.  I plan to fill a 1/2 cup measuring cup with a spoon small enough to fit in the jar.
Switch out the peach preserves for apricot and add some dehydrated onion and you have my husband’s Grandma Cele’s classic chicken marinade!
I had my bachelor party dinner at Princess Pamela’s in 1995.  Best soul food ever and boy was that the most well behaved bachelor party you could ever imagine.
What kind of vinegar did Pamela use?
unless otherwise noted assume it is white vinegar
@susan I bet it would be good with apple cider vinegar.
I made this while on vacation in  Nice France.  I served  this with a duck breast over red and white rice.  I used a lime instead of lemon and used balsamic vinegar.   It was absolutely sublime!
This works well in the places you’d use chutney. Great for swirl on top of bean-veg-grain bowls.
You would use 8 tablespoons in a recipe rather than 1/2 cup because you can scoop the preserves out of the jar with the tablespoon
I used to eat there… it was freaky dining in a restaurant on the second floor of a brick building in the East Village. Divine chicken.
@Sally. I loved that place. She used to bring me extras when I was a starving student in the 70’s
I've made a sauce similar to this for years. Instead of lemon juice, substitute soy sauce. Wonderful with grilled pork tenderloin and chicken.

I cannot tell how thrilled I am to try this! I actually went to Princess Pamela's (probably in the early 80's). A jazz-guitar playing friend knew about it and knew Princess Pamela's boyfriend, who was a bass player. 

It was QUITE the experience and yes, you had the feeling you might get kicked out any time (we weren't) but the food was great. If I remember correctly, Princess Pamela's mother was also there.

I have fond memories of Princess Pamela and her restaurant. She was host, server, and entertainer at once. As host, as you write, she'd determine if you were welcome in. As server, she would rattle off the fixed menu of the evening then ask "now what are you having?" As entertainer, patrons would beg, "please, Princess Pamela, won't you sing for us" - she'd play shy, then give in - her cook would come out and string the bass while she serenaded the small dining room.

I made this and I liked how it turned out, but I think the recipe should include some measurements of salt, pepper, and Worcestershire sauce because I added a considerable amount of all three. I was born and raised in the Deep South, so any Southern food/soul food recipe that calls for black pepper is asking for a generous amount. And given all the sugar in the recipe, the salt and Worcestershire sauce are going to be used in liberal amounts to balance out the flavors.
This sauce is absolutely delicious. It went perfectly with salmon, and I can't wait to make it with fried chicken.

More Princess Pamela recipes please. I’ve lost my cookbook. Still live around the corner from her 10th St restaurant, what a rickety building that was but the food was fabulous. Pamela’s and the Pink Teacup in the West Village were my introduction to soul food.
Hope fig jam works too!

I was a regular at both places she had. The first was over a Thai restaurant (maybe 10th Street), whose owners she called "the Formosians," mistaking Thailand for Taiwan.  She was quite a character, and would often sit at our table and regale us with stories. The second was the one on Houston Street. It was usually two choices, and the sides were the same whichever you chose. Great food, and fond memories of the waitress she had, who would shuffle around in a starched 1960s uniform and wig.
I remember eating Princess Pamela’s smothered pork chops when I lived in the east village. A rare treat!

Thank you!  I have a 1969 copy of Princess Pamela's Soul Food Cookbook. I just now noticed that it is autographed.   The cover is missing, the back page is torn off and the pages are yellowed. I  gave away 50 cookbooks and kept 5.  This one because of the scribble scrabble on the shrimp boil page, the memories and the Milk Baked Ham recipe.   Thank you!
Glorious memories of Princess Pamela’s, but can’t say the food was at the top of the list! Oh but the experience was not to be beat.
@TPJ You didn’t rate the food top of the list because you thought there was better soul food to be had, or because it wasn’t a type of cuisine you particularly enjoyed? Genuinely curious.

Was excited to try this on a pork tenderloin: VERY sweet. Dialed that down with a splash of soy but wished I had ignored the “add a half cup of water” note. There’s oil in here and you can see the separation, plus it’s as thin as consommés. I’d hold off and add water as needed.
Can Worcestershire be omitted? I have never been able to acquire the taste for it. Are there any substitutions that could work?
Sherry - You ask if Worcestershire sauce can be omitted.  Sure!  The recipe says "to taste" for amount, so leaving it out works fine.

I made this and it was tasty, but very thin. Following directions to add 1/2 cup water and then I let it boil/simmer forever.  It never thickened.
I guess I would make again, but maybe not add the full amount of water, or even at all.
What do you think of this as a pulled pork barbeque sauce?

I am looking, in vain, for some kind of article about Princess Pamela's attached to this lone recipe. It was a rather strange spot, in which her "guests" were locked in after gaining admittance, and whose food was...err...secondary to the experience. It was all a bit, well eccentric is a kind word, but scary is perhaps more accurate.
Can fresh peaches be substituted for preserved?

@Selden
I wouldn’t think that fresh peaches would be a good substitute for peach jam.
It's not "preserved peaches." It's "peach preserves." Preserves are in the same category as jams, jellies and marmalades.

I didn't have peach preserves but did have apricot preserves so I substituted them for the peach. Other than that, everything was as written. We loved the sauce and had it with baked pork chops and quinoa.   I think it will be good with a lot of different dishes.

Vis-a-vis jam: the easiest thing is to look at the jam label.  See how much a serving (1 tbsp) weighs, then weigh that x8 right into your saucepan.  In fact, weighing any ingredients right into the recipe saves washing measuring cups, spoons, etc.  More accurate, too.  
BTW, a quickie sauce with a very similar result.  Equal parts jam and prepared mustard heated with a little butter right into the microwave makes a delicious sauce for baked chicken, pork chops, fish, etc. etc. in about 2 minutes
This was watery and never thickened despite boiling a very long time. I used Bon Maman peach preserves. I’m thinking the jam used in the original recipe was a thicker preserve. Maybe Smuckers?
Eight tablespoons of most fruit jams are usually @120 g by Weight.

In the 80’s we had Miss Anne’s in Fort Greene on South Oxford, just at the top of the Lafayette C subway station steps. It was a lower level take out in a house … no choices just what she made that day. I don’t know if it was as good as PP’s but we thought it was the real deal.
What are peach preserves? Jam?
Basically. In general, preserves have bigger chunks of fruit than jam, but they're pretty interchangeable.

To SA - I wouldn't 'pour' most jams, but suppose I'd spoon it into a measuring cup, so for 8 Tbsp, might as well do that than spoon into a half cup measure. 
This sounds like a delightful sauce
Oh my, what great memories...thanks for sharing!
This recipe looks like it could be canned safely. Has anyone ever tried?
What kind of vinegar??? There are so many to choose from. I’m not a huge fan of vinegar. The smell of it while I was pregnant still haunts me. Any advice?

Lisa - You ask the good question of what kind of vinegar since there are so many.  My choice here is usually Katz brand apple cider vinegar but I think any good AC vinegar would work.  I also like to use Katz late harvest  sauvignon blanc vinegar in things like this.
@Lisa   Good sherry vinegar has a gentle scent
This was very good, but next time I'll add little or no water. Since the recipe doesn't contain cornstarch or flour, the thickening is just boiling down the water. Why bother?
@Sherry - Pickapeppa sauce makes a satisfactory anchovy-free Worcestershire sauce substitute.
I wish they gave an approximate time for cooking!!
I made this with apricot jam and served it with pork tenderloin. So delicious
Delicious. I used a jar of homemade peach preserves and sherry vinegar. We all enjoyed over brined, grilled pork chops. Very pleased and I will definitely make again.
Just made a big batch and froze some. Hoping it will not affect quality.
It didn't thicken when I cooked it, just kept evaporating, I guess along with some good taste. It tasted like warm peach soup. Too watery.
It would be really helpful to have a recipe for epis spice here, since the recipe mentions it can be homemade...
You should have included the recipe for epis (Haitian seasoning). I know I can google it.  I want the one that would be recommended.

In the recipe description, you will see thst "epis" is underlined.  The underline indicates that this is a link to the recipe.  Click it and it will take you to the NYTimes Cooking recipe for epis, which had been previously published.
There's a hot link in the intro with a recipe
Just curious, what’s the point of the 10 - 12 pieces of macaroni? (With 4 servings, that would be ~3 pieces each.)
@Amy yes puzzling to me as well
So... how does one make epis?
NYTimes Cooking recipe for Epis is here: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021894-haitian-epis-pepper-herb-and-garlic-marinade?q=epi
A little question: what is the purpose of adding macaroni to the sauce?

Meatballs were lovely, light and flavorful, but too spicy, even for us who are accustomed to quite spicy food.  I had a jar of jamaican epis in my pantry so that’s what I used.  Seven tablespoons seemed like a lot for a pound of meat.  I too wondered what purpose the macaroni was supposed to serve, and I left it out of the accompanying sauce, which was delicious and not too spicy.  I might try broiling/baking the meatballs when I try it again.  Frying was very messy and used an excessive amount of oil, in my opinion.
I shared this recipe with a friend who is Haitian and he was surprised by the 7 TBS of Epis. It seemed like a lot to him. Is it correct? Thanks
I'm reluctant to fry anything, can these be baked? our gently sautéed or simmered?S
I'm thinking the same thing, would probably cook them in my air frier.

I made mine gluten free. I used a gluten free baguette--DON'T DO IT. The coconut milk made it mush.  HOWEVER, the meatballs came out fine with the white mush added. I rolled in brown rice flour and that absorbed some wetness. Reduced the epis in the meatballs to 5 T, per other suggestions that they were too spicy. The spice level was fine, as they still had bite and the sauce was spicy, too. I also cooked the sauce down about 30 minutes after adding the onions and they were still crisp.

A few modifications this time: gluten free breadcrumbs in the meatballs, browning them in only 1-2 T olive oil and keeping them warm while I make the sauce. Because the sauce is meant to be watery, I cook it down for at least 30 minutes, adding the meatballs to cook through and the sliced onions. Threw in handfuls of spinach on the side and cooked for a just a minute or so. Was excellent served with basmati rice and sauce over everything. And the epis is great to use in other cooking.

Collards are a great candidate for an instant pot, and there's no reason not to include the stems. I separate and dice the stems and give them a head start by sauteing them with the onions. Add the liquid and simmer for a few minutes (part of the head start). Then add the greens and wilt, pop on the lid, and cook under pressure for about 10 minutes. This also gets more flavor out of the turkey or ham, especially if you include the bones.
Where do you get a smoked turkey wing? Not what I want to cook. The rest, yes.
In the grocery store, typically found next to hams.
Almost any lchain grocery store in the smoked meat section, usually at one end of their meat shelving.
You can find packaged smoked turkey wings in the meat section, along with ham hocks and a range of other smoked meats.
After making the soft-boiled eggs, peel and marinade in soy sauce for a day before slicing and putting atop the ramen soup. Tastier and better looking among the other toppings.

From the article it seems like the dish calls for marinated eggs. This other ramen recipe includes those: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1024748-shoyu-ramen?algo=identity&fellback=true&imp_id=4581972522193392&req_id=976859236439621&surface=cooking-search-web&variant=0_relevance_reranking
This sounds wonderful. Collard Greens with a smoked turkey wing is  the greatest.
Why do you need to cook the ramen separately? Can’t you just put them in the broth?

@Andante cooking noodles releases starches into the water. Cooking them in the broth will affect the look and possibly texture of the soup. Also, if you don’t plan to eat the soup all at once, leaving the noodles in the broth will affect their texture. If none of this is an issue for you, go ahead!

The potlikker was a revelation of umami yumminess! I used smoked turkey breast and substituted avocado oil for most of the butter. The recipe made a very generous dinner for two, with some broth and collards left over.
I made as directed, except added ginger, a jalapeno, and white miso paste.    My whole family loved it!
For just 2 servings you use a quart of chicken broth and another quart of water?
I can only find smoked turkey legs locally. Will that suffice or too little fat?

@Hulahome turkey legs actually have more fat than wings. The benefit of the wing usually in this sort of instance is collagen, to give the broth more body and silkiness. A leg will lend plenty of flavor all the same.
Yes. Smoked turkey legs are just fine.
If you can't get smoked turkey or ham raw where you live, would smoked bacon and mostly skinless chicken wings (for bones) suffice?

I think that would be fine. The collard greens component of this dish is commonly made with salt pork instead of smoked turkey, which is very similar to bacon. Other good substitutes would be ham hock, smoked turkey necks, or a whole turkey drumstick - and I think chicken would be fine in place of turkey.
Isn't potliqquer also the broth left after cooking dried beans?   That's what I've always called it.

This was okay, have had better ramen - though this is pretty easy.  Flavor from the smoked turkey was good in the broth but didn't care for the texture of the meat and would have preferred something more tender.  Maybe smoked chicken?
I am in Australia collard greens are unknown here what would be a good alternative

@Anne in the UK they’re helpfully called “greens” or “spring greens” when the vegetables are more tender in Spring. I only knew they were the same thing because of google images haha. Otherwise look for leafy vegetables that don’t taste like cabbage or bitter like escarole type thing.

Turnip greens are a decent substitute, if you can get them. You could probably use some types of kale (any tougher variety that you would put in a soup or stew) - it will have a different flavor from collard greens, but the method would be the same.

Made as written but with smoked turkey necks because that's what we had.
What a treat! Absolutely staved off a cold winter day.

this was delicious!! used a turkey leg as wings were unavailable near me. Topped with chili oil, fried shallots, and eggs marinated in sake, mirin, and soy sauce. The potlikker and greens reminded me a lot of caldo galego, and I'll probably add some potatoes and white beans to the leftover broth!

Finally a ramen for the likes of me:  Japanese/Hillbilly Fusion!  @Name's comment made me realize this might also be good with shredded cabbage, although that might give it a kind of Eastern European flair -- which could also be a good thing, but would render the name "potlikker" untenable, as I think that really only applies to the leafy greens.

Delicious! I couldn't find a smoked turkey wing, so I used a smoked turkey drum. I used the Instant Pot, and the whole thing came together in about an hour. After completing step 2, I moved everything to the IP and set it on low pressure for 15 minutes. Once the pressure was released, I used the sauté mode and cooked the defrosted noodles directly in the broth. The turkey drum added a lot of flavor.

My Tennessee grandmother cooked greens for dinner at least once a week, and we grandkids waited around the stove until they were done, when she would portion out little glasses of potlikker for us to drink.  Does this recipe work with thick-sliced bacon, pork belly, or ham hock instead of smoked turkey wing?  Does it work with kale, mustard greens, Swiss chard, or beet greens?  I'd love to try a variation that would remind me of my grandmother's no-recipe-just-what-feels-right version.
Great flavor from the smoked turkey wing! The collard greens were also a hit. Two items I’ve never tried but will definitely be used again.
I think it would do well with some thyme
OK, I tried it. Disgustingly sweet, unrefined and childish, except that kids cannot drink this (but neither should anyone else).

In case anyone else needs this information.

Benedictine and Green Chartreuse are both herbal French liqueurs, with Benedictine having a slightly sweeter, more balanced flavor profile, while Green Chartreuse is known for its strong, herbaceous, and slightly peppery taste due to its higher alcohol content and more potent herbal blend; both are traditionally produced by monks in their respective monasteries. 

I would definitely go with the sweeter one.

I’ve tried several variations to make it less sweet.. this one seems to be the best: 
1 1/2 Oz Pistachio Liquor
1 oz Vodka
1/4 oz Vanilla Vodka 
1/4 oz Green Chartreuse 
No heavy whipping cream.. imo it’s not needed the pistachio crème liquor is enough.

I had a “Pistachio” martini recently that left me confused and amazed.  Equal parts Baileys, Disaronno, and cheap Blue Curaçao.  Tasted like jello pistachio pudding. I don’t know how and I’m still confused
I had several pistachio martinis in Boston and none of them required pistachio liquor. Some combo of Baileys, Frangelico, vanilla vodka…much more common ingredients. Give it a google!
@Jaimee how is that pistachio tho ?

@Lisa

Surprisingly, tastes exactly like pistachio

@Jaimee

It’s bc almond is actually the primary flavor in both Jello brand pistachio pudding and most pistachio ice creams. Our brains perceive the almond flavor as pistachio

I could not find pistachio liqueur so I substituted Frangelico. In addition, I misread recipe and used 2x amount of Benedictine. We absolutely loved the drink. Our pistachio nuts were roasted and salted…again the only type I could fine. The drink was outstanding and a perfect Christmas Eve treat, DIL who has taste more toward a desert drink said it was one of the best cocktails she had ever had if not the best. So fun for a special ocassion.
Delicious, even without the gelato and the rim.
I made it exactly as written and it was delicious.  I used green chartreuse, hold the gelato. Volatile yet smooth.  What a treat! For context, my usual drink is a perfect Manhattan, up.

I made this with Borgata pistachio liqueur, Veil vanilla vodka and Benedictine. The Borgata was what I could find and is delicious, but very very sweet. We drank them and enjoyed them, but depending on the sweetness of your pistachio liqueur I would consider using plain vodka instead of vanilla vodka. If I make them again I will use plain vodka.

I was planning on making this drink for New Year's Eve, but was unable to find the pistachio liqueur in the Hudson Valley.  I stopped at three stores, then gave up.  I will stick with my tried and true chocolate espresso martini!

Had to replace Benedictine with B B which is think is much sweeter than as written. But still delicious! Very pistachio and not too heavy even for summer drinks. Going to look for other ways to use this pistachio liqueur too
Ignore JV, this is delicious!
Replace cream with lime juice, add egg white.  Shake vigorously.  Light and delicious.
My recipe is just 1.5 oz vodka, 1.5 oz. Frangelico, and 1.5 oz pistachio cream liqueur. The booziness and the sweetness balance out evenly.

title: Crispy Trout With Creek Sauce Recipe
author: Korsha Wilson

comments.commentBody: Can one forgo the rice flour and up either regular flour or cornmeal? With what, if any, adverse impact?

This was a great, easy recipe. I subbed rockfish for the trout because that is what I had, but otherwise made as written. The flour mix made a thin but crunchy coating and the fish was tender. The creek sauce, while similar to tartar sauce, was so much better and will be my go to from now on.

I subbed additional cornmeal for the rice flour and double fried the trout since we only had about a pound. The sauce is fantastic, we will definitely be using it for all our future fish catch and fries in the future.
That Creek sauce is absolutely wonderful. I wish it kept longer than 4 days. I used Hellman's and wonder if Dukes would have been even better.
This was terrific. I didn't have corn meal, so I had to use semolina. It was still delicious. This will go into the rotation.  otherwise I followed the recipe, and did my own filleting of the trout.

I didn't know: Putting the butter in the freezer helps ensure it remains very cold until added to the sauce at the end of cooking. When cold butter is incorporated into a hot sauce slowly and gradually, it emulsifies better, creating a smoother, silkier sauce with a velvety texture. If the butter is too warm or melted when added, it can separate, making the sauce greasy instead of creamy. Freezing the butter briefly ensures optimal emulsification, resulting in a rich, glossy finish to your sauce

@Rick H
Bravo Rick, you thoroughly answered m question about frozen butter going into a hot pan. Not even Google AI had an answer!

@Rick H,

I didn’t know this either. You’re my NYT cooking Hero of the Week.
Hmmm.  Wonder if I can use Old Bay Hot Sauce instead of Tabasco because Old Bay makes the world go round
Old Bay is a cayenne hot sauce, which is what the recipe calls for (Tabasco brand was used only as an parenthetical example)

@Terps Fan
Great idea! I’ll try it next time.

I cut the tabasco to make it edible for my wife, served it over cavatelli, and it was a hit with the family. I doubled the reciped and probably used about 40 percent of the Tabasco called for (slightly under one tablespoon instead of two full ones). I'm a big fan of heat and it had a satisfying fire wthout feeling the need to add any additional Tabasco, so those with a sensitive food palate might want to go easy on the Tabasco, at least on their first try.

We loved this dish to pieces, but it is quite rich. Thinking it would be too spicy, I cut the Tabasco in half and finished the sauce, but then added the rest of the Tabasco after tasting. The sauce was sufficient for another half-pound of shrimp. Next time we may try cutting the butter to two Tbsp. and/or using half and half instead of cream. Also, the cooking times are too long for tender shrimp, so I advise less time for the initial sauté and a quick warm-up at the end. Crusty sourdough was perfect for sopping, but we might serve over steamed greens next time.

@Mark in Lacey good tips! I loved this but would love to make modifications so I could have it on a heavier rotation! Was also trying to think of alternatives to the cream and I think with half the butter it would still be great. 

Oh yes - I made this exactly per the recipe and loved it!

Has anyone made this as called for in the recipe? I’m interested in your reaction to it as the thought of a TABLESPOON of Tabasco in a dish with pound of shrimp and sauce sounds a bit like a death wish.

@Doug C. It’s hard to know how to respond to this because it wasn’t too bad for me…but then again I have friends who can’t take any heat and could never have eaten this. I can’t eat the very hot salsa that’s for sale in the grocery store where I live in Texas…this was definitely hot but not inedibly so for me. Actually I think the heat was the pepper as much as the Tabasco. It was fab though we were licking the plate.

@Doug C. I’ve not made it….yet, plan to & plan to use the full amount of tobacco b/c I surmise (from experience) that the tabasco‘s heat will end up cut considerably by the heavy cream. granted I LOVE hot food :)

I made this today for lunch. It is my kind of recipe- flavorful, quick and easy, and made of ingredients I had in the pantry. I was able to cook all of the shrimp in 1 tablespoon of olive oil in the original browning phase. I also used sriracha - about 3/4 tablespoon - because that is what I had in the house. Black pepper is pleasingly prominent. Served over a baked potato. Will definitely make again!

I used dry vermouth for the wine, whole milk yogurt for the cream, half a white onion for shallot. Could have used more shrimp. I had an Italian hot sauce with Calabria chilies and only used 1 tbsp of worstie. finished with Parm regg. Yum.
Thank you for the completely different recipe.
@J Pls tell me @ yogurt instead of cream. When did you add? Did it separate? I’m averse to so much cream. Thx
Used about 3/4 tbsp Tabasco and it wasn’t too spicy.  And used chunks of chicken breast instead of shrimp.  Will make it this way again.
