I can’t wait to try this sauce with chicken or pork and some linguine! It’s possibilities are endless…@c.boyardee

Made this with Franks Red Hot Sauce and found it had the perfect amount of heat. Used 1.17 lb shrimp and 10 oz of scallop without adjusting other ingredients and found it sauce-y enough since the extra protein also brought moisture. Served with toasted sourdough, roasted green beans and broccolini. Looking forward to repeating this one.

We really liked it and added more Tabasco and crushed red peppers. I added more cream than called for as hubby loves sopping up sauce with bread. Definitely would be great for a crowd if you doubled it. All you need is some bread and a salad.

I used 1/2 the hot sauce called for and although I didn’t think the full measure would’ve been too hot, it delightfully allowed the black pepper to come through a little bit more prominently. I served it over pasta. Make sure you’ve got your mise en place as the wonderful sauce and this dish comes together quite quickly.

I made this exactly as stated and it was pretty much perfect. A hair rich for my taste, but a lovely treat. Eat a salad in advance of this so that you aren’t quite as tempted to drink the sauce.

We ate this over roasted potatoes and it was divine, but I suspect anything would be delicious with this.
Meant to say A Hit.

Excellent and delicious. Trader Joes has a frozen "seafood blend" which includes shrimp, calamari and scallops which worked beautifully. Didn't have a shallot on hand so subbed half a diced yellow onion. Used 1/4 t dried rosemary and 1/2 t dried thyme (I'm sure the fresh would be better but . . . life.)

This recipe is going in my repeat box! We did cut the Tabasco in half because we have spice averse people and it was delicious even for them. The cream cuts the spice a ton. Served it with crusty bread but over mashed potatoes or pasta would be great too.

Added a handful of frozen spinach leftover from something else. It went in at the same time as the hot sauce.  Added a pretty green color and herbaceous quality.  We really loved this and will make it again.

A really yummy shrimp dish! I served it with brown rice, which was the perfect complement to the creamy sauce. I didn't have Worcestershire sauce (forgot I ran out), so just left it out, and I imagine it would only improve this very delicious dish. The only thing I would do differently is make more sauce!

In Texas and Louisiana we use a full tablespoon of Tabasco.
New Englanders, you might want to use a dash. 
I learned the hard way, do not use 1/2 and 1/2 if you don’t have heavy cream, it’s too light and will separate. 
Another good but overly complicated NY Times recipe.  Cutting butter into chunks and freezing it?  Really?  Let’s eat!
The overall concept is good, just run with it and improvise. 
Served over rice.
I was just wondering about using half and half. Will now go to the store for heavy cream.
Really easy and yummy!! We are a high spice household so I ended up doubling the Tabasco and pepper.
I find it simpler to cook the shrimp in the sauce at the end.

Hi Judith, I have not made this yet but  am leery of recipes that call for cooking shrimp until almost done, removing them, making a sauce, then adding the shrimp back into the pan. 
 Seems to me to be a recipe for overcooked shrimp. Did you add the shrimp right before you added the butter (which is what I was thinking about doing as I read the recipe) or afterwards?
I made this exactly as written.  Such an amazing, delicious and easy recipe.
Great flavors BUT don't need to cook the shrimp first (as they got a bit rubbery); next time we will cook the shrimp in the sauce.

This was outstanding! I did not have a shallot and used 1/4 of a sweet onion. I also used dried rosemary and thyme. Doubled Tabasco as well. Served with some crusty garlic bread and I will be making this again.

Putting the butter in the freezer helps ensure it remains very cold until added to the sauce at the end of cooking. When cold butter is incorporated into a hot sauce slowly and gradually, it emulsifies better, creating a smoother, silkier sauce with a velvety texture. If the butter is too warm or melted when added, it can separate, making the sauce greasy instead of creamy. Freezing the butter briefly ensures optimal emulsification, resulting in a rich, glossy finish to your sauce

Oh, my.

As we were eating this, my husband looked at me with gravitas and said, “Please save this to the recipe file.”
It was great and easy! I did not have Tabasco so i used Franks hot sauce.

Admittedly I didn’t have several of the sauce ingredients so had to improvise, eg gin instead of white wine. However, I thought the thick creamy sauce overpowered the shrimp which has a delicate flavour. I think it would be ok on steak.
Cut back on the butter and cream as others suggested. Next time I’ll only u
Soooo yummy with a hot, crunchy loaf of bread.
While we were eating, Kevin couldn’t stop yelling about how good this is.
Delish. Great new take on shrimp and rice. I replaced the heavy cream with coconut cream.
Excellent. Used only one mini bottle of Tabasco. Husband thought spice level was perfect. Used water and a little additional lemon juice instead of wine.

This was delicious with just slight modifications. I didn’t have fresh herbs so used 1/2 tsp of both rosemary and thyme. Also, I used half and half instead of cream (what I had on hand). I added the 2 TBSP of butter at the end and it didn’t need another 2 to thicken it. The 1 TBSP hot sauce was enough for my husband but I added more. We ate it on pasta and it really was delicious. Next time I will add cooked asparagus or other green vegetable to help sop up the sauce and instead of pasta try a really good crusty bread. So many possibilities!  Thanks for this recipe!

This did NOT work for us. Made as written, and the sauce basically just tasted like Worcestershire sauce. I did like the cook on the shrimp- they were perfect. But next time I’ll just improv a garlic butter sauce…this recipe just wasn’t appealing enough to try again.
This. Dish. SLAPS! It’s so flavorful, so easy, so complex in flavor… I like it with gnocchi.

This recipe will be in my regular rotation!! My husband doesn’t do dairy so I subbed plant butter sticks and cream of coconut milk (coco lopez) to make it non-dairy. Deliciousness!! You could also use full fat coconut milk - just chill the can in fridge ahead of time so the fat is at the top. I used the full tbsp of Frank’s hot sauce and that combo with the coconut lopez was perfect for me. I like a hint of spice that lingers as you eat.
I made this with the full Tablespoon of Tabasco, and it had a slight kick, but I didn’t think it was too hot at all! Very tasty!
Made exactly as the recipe instructs, and my son and I think it’s perfect.  We ladle the sauce over blanched green beans, the shrimp, and dip everything up with a baguette.
Why does the butter go in the freezer?
So the sauce doesn’t break (separate) as easily.
I believe it's so that it will mostly emulsify in the sauce, thickening it slowly, rather than just adding it for taste. I've seen this technique before.

I believe freezing the butter makes it easier tp emulsify it smoothly into the sauce.  Freezing the butter allows it to slowly mix in with the rest of the sauce.  Using cold butter and adding it in small amounts at a time is another method often used.
I used Nando‘s hot sauce, otherwise followed the recipe. It was s hit.
Used water, a half teaspoon of sugar and a little additional lemon instead of wine. Used half the amount of hot sauce and heat level was good for my spouse.

OK, Texan here - just adding Tabasco sauce does not BBQ it make! (Said like Moira from Schitt’s Creek) Use a tablespoon of real spicy BBQ sauce (or more) and it is delicious! I tried with beer too instead of wine and also not bad. Overall really like this recipe because love shrimp!

@Janet W It’s not BBQ in the traditional sense. The New Orleans style BBQ shrimp that this recipe is inspired by is shrimp cooked with butter, Worcestershire sauce, and peppers, both black and red. I don’t know why it’s named that but it’s a very traditional Creole recipe.

Definitely do not follow the recipe quantity for the hot sauce--it was inedible w/the spice level.  Overall--a rather unimaginative flavor provile--the hot sauce and pepper overwhelms everything else--also not sure what makes this "BBQ."  Do not recommend.
This based on a classic dish from NoLa that is called BBQ shrimp. It has all of the major elements and is served across the city.

Not sure what I did wrong but I ended up with way too much sauce, it was too heavy with the cream and I would use a quarter of the Worcestershire sauce. There was nothing BBQ y about it but I defense of the recipes I think the author was only alluding to BBQ. Not going to try this again sadly. On to the next new recipe, however.

@Victoria A It’s not traditional BBQ they’re referring to—the dish is based on New Orleans style BBQ shrimp which is not like BBQ very much at all— it’s shrimp cooked with a lot of butter, Worcestershire sauce, and pepper.
Used 1 tsp Tabasco and that was plenty. Unless you like fire, start with less and then you can always add more.
Made this last week and it's outstanding. This is going into the rotation.

This was excellent. I served it over noodles with the shrimp and sauce. I skipped the Tabasco and added a few drops of franks hot sauce. The frozen butter is a great addition I would not have thought of to use. Worcestershire was an odd ingredient to me but it worked well. I will definitely make this again.

This was absolutely delicious. I did cut back on the Tabasco because I don’t handle spice well, but did enough to give it a little kick. Served it with some homemade crusty bread. Will definitely be making this again.
We thought we could have used more of the sauce.  It was enough for 2 but not 4.

The spice level in this recipe is great and well-balanced. A tbsp of hot sauce may sound like a lot, but it's competing with 1/2c of cream and 1/2 stick of butter. I hedged my bets and did 50/50 Tabasco and Cholula, which was delicious. May try others in the future!
Cooked this on January 11, '25. Loved it! I used Sriracha for the hot sauce, great taste.

Followed the recipe pretty exactly but only had 3/4 pound of shrimp for 2 people. (A pound for four seems a bit meager?) Did cut the butter in half and the sauce was plenty rich.  Served over Basmati rice. Could use a sprinkle of chopped Italian parsley or other fresh herb on top but fine without it.
Use Frank's or Louisana - anything but Tabasco and heat profile goes down alot .......not to mention taste better.

The Lobster Pot in P-Town. My first job after my freshman year at Boston University aka BU.
When Dick LaBrie owned it, Before Chef Tim McNulty and this fabulous recipe.

This recipe is delicious. It is a very truncated version of (imho) the standard for all NOLA bbq shrimp, Emeril’s. (That recipe can be found in his first cookbook.) It is a bit light on the bay laurel/creole flavor of Emeril’s, but it is still Worcestershire-forward enough to be authentic.
The butter is frozen so it will melt slowly and fully incorporate into the sauce.

I made this according to the recipe, served it over cavatappi and was very happy with the results. I would like to propose a new name: Astonishingly Delicious Creamy Spicy Shrimp. That should take care of any barbecue confusion.
This is absolutely divine and don’t change a thing. Crusty warm baguette is all you need on the side (along with Sauvignon Blanc)!

This is a wonderful recipe. As a first-timer, I followed it exactly. I noted that it called for one tablespoon of hot sauce, not two and two tablespoons of Worcestershire sauce. This gave it a very pleasant, mild heat.
A word to the wise - this comes together very quickly once the shrimp are browned so you absolutely must have your mise en place ready to go. Then it’s really easy! Lovely dinner!
Made exactly as written...easy and excellent. Served with polenta cakes. A keeper!

My cream had gone bad so used 1/2 and 1/2 and it came out just fine.  Oh, also, I forgot to add the black pepper which I think will take it to next level but everyone thought it perfect.  I did 1.5 of the recipe and it was scant for 4 people, recommend doubling for a party of 4.  YUM!
This recipe is a WINNER! No reason to cook the shrimp first. I added just after the cream and before the butter. Absolutely delicious! (I knew I had to try it when I saw it was from the Lobster Pot!)
The shrimp is WAAAY better with a sear on it.
Not for the fat phobic but definitely worthy of the five star rating. Served with pasta but I think rice might work even better.

I had to modify quite a bit to work with what I had on hand. I had 3 lbs shrimp but not enough ingredients to 3x the recipe so I doubled the sauce (still plenty of sauce). I used shiner beer instead of wine, onion instead of shallot. Only 1 tbsp Tabasco. I added black pepper to taste (lots of it, but not 3 tsp). Dry herbs in place of fresh. Used cajun seasoning on the shrimp which added extra heat. Served with sourdough bread. Husband said it was a top 3 dinner. It's going in the permanent file.
Made this today, was one of the tastiest sauces I’ve ever had. Used a full tablespoon of Franks Red Hot, because that’s what I had, and it was perfect (but I like spicy).

I have made this dish twice now and I really do enjoy it. Few dishes use cooking the shrimp to impart the shrimp flavor into the dish for depth of flavor like this one does. Adding a glug more of cream to the sauce (if you need to) anytime after it starts cooking will not hurt the sauce at all. This is really nice served over polenta.
I followed the recipe and the results were DELICIOUS. A dish to be made again and gain.

I’ve pretty much cut dairy fats out of my diet per Dr. order, so heavy cream is not really an option for me, though I could use half of the butter called for. I’m wondering about using full fat coconut milk instead of the cream. I realize this will completely change the flavor of the recipe, but it seems to me it would still be delicious. Thoughts?

@Mark I subbed coco lopez and used plant base butter and it was still delicious. Full fat coconut would work, too, and likely not be as sweet as the coco lopez but I found the combo of the sweetness with the spice was great for me.
@Nikki in DC  Thanks Nikki!
Very tasty and easily adapted to suit ingredients on hand. Served it with rice. I'll make it again.

I usually tweak recipes - this one is perfect and fantastic as written. The amount of tabasco, Worcestershire and ground pepper works perfectly. I use a bit less pepper if I don't have the coarse ground version. I find the heat in the low-medium zone (the cream and butter tone it way down) and for our family I tend to add more tabasco/Frank's. Excellent sauce even for chicken. We serve with bread to mop up the sauce or over pasta. I usually triple the recipe for our big clan and it is a hit!

This was delicious! I used a pound of shrimp and a pound of scallops, doubled the sauce ingredients but did 1 3/4 the amount of butter. It fed four of us perfectly. I guess the appetites are larger in our house.  Also I just added the shrimp and scallops to the sauce per the other comments (no cooking ahead of the sauce) and cooked on super low for maybe 5 minutes covered, stirring occasionally. Was definitely spicy but we like spice. Race reviews from the group, even my husband who isn’t wild about shrimp.
The sauce was incredible, but only when paired with the shrimp and bread. It is a punch in the face on its own but a flavor explosion while accompanied.
Why do recipes insist on leaving the tails on shrimp? Is the visual worth the extra work for the diner?

@Tucker
Tails add flavor to the sauce.
What if I left out the heavy cream?  Trying to cut calories and I'm a lactode!
@Joan E. I’ve had success with coconut milk or canned coconut cream in other recipes. I’m going to give that a try with this one.
Where is the BBQ part?

@Cory Remjeske The “BBQ” part is just an old New Orleans naming convention.The original creator of the dish thought the sauced shrimp looked like barbecue, hence the name. To be fair, most NOLA interpretations are more Worcestershire-intense, and the shells are left on, so it kinda made sense.
I could add this comment to a million recipes; my wife is allergic to garlic. Is there anything I can use as a substitute?

I know nothing about garlic allergy so this might be silly, but is she also allergic to garlic powder?  You might try minced fresh ginger instead.  Completely different flavor than garlic, but might also be delicious, and adds a similar slight "sting" on the palate.
I can't eat garlic either and use garlic infused olive oil. The part I am sensitive to is not fat soluble.
I really dislike Tabasco, or any sauce with a lot of vinegar, so I tripled the amount of black pepper,  and it came out great.
Why freeze the butter?
Any ideas of what to sub for the cream if you're lactose intollerant?

@RP
Coconut cream is my substitute of choice. I am lactose intolerant too. Alternatively Cocojune coconut yogurt  or Wegmans brand coconut yogurt.
A tablespoon of tabasco might be too much.
Basically a riff on shrimp etoufee.
Where is the BBQ in this?
Why is it called BBQ shrimps? What’s BBQ about this recipe?

The frozen butter chunks melt more slowly and blend into the sauce well
 Others have explained it bett r r in other comments.  @Claire Duncan
I used chili crisp ..... YUM!

The dish was great, got the highest rating ever from my wife. 
However, cooking times for shrimp were WAY too long. Recipe called for cooking the shrimp 2-3 minutes per side. I cooked less than a minute per side and the shrimp in the finished dish bordered on overcooked.

It was perfect! My only suggestion is: I assumed that the way they had you cook the shrimp, that they should only be mostly cooked. But they didn't quite cook through when added to the skillet on low. So cook them until they are just cooked through, or when you put the shrimp back in, turn the heat back up to medium to let them finish.

This came across on my NYT recipe sub today and decided to make this for dinner instead of ordering Friday night pizza…boy…sure glad I did. It was excellent. Only used about 9 very large/jumbo shrimp for 2 but kept the sauce quantities the same. Used Chipotle Tabasco and just under a tablespoon. The sauce flavors are outstanding. Served with warmed slices of Kalamata and Green Olive bread and Green Salad with Sour Cream and Onion Dressing. We were discussing how left over shrimp on a slice of bread, sauce over and melted cheese might mingle…hmmm.

I made it exactly as written and it was excellent.  Too fatty for a regular night at home, but perfect for company when you want something fairly easy and quick.  Served with crusty bread and hubby would have like pasta.
Thanks for this excellent, elegant recipe. We’ve passed by the Lobster Pot numerous times over the years but never ate there. Now we’ve eaten there vicariously!

I used a tbsp of  Crystal hot sauce instead of Tabasco because I was out. Crystal is still a cayenne sauce - but not as hot. It was perfect. I did everything else per the recipe.  I will make it again.

Made This Dish, Tonite, Pretty Much as Written (although I Did Cut the Tabasco sauce to 1/2 tbs), I Was Only Preparing 1/2 lb. of Shrimp, And My Tolerance For Spicy/Hot is on the Lower End. The Sauce is on the Velvety, Rich Side, But Complex w/  Layers of Flavor. Both the Cream and the Butter at the End Absolutely Quiet Down the Heat. Definitely Reminded Me of a New Orleans/Creole Dish You'd Find in the French Quarter. As One Reader Noted, Important to Have Everything in Place before you Begin.

Use Cholula instead of Tobasco. Less than 1Tbsp also.  Cholula has flavor as well as enough heat. I am a former big Tobasco fan, my daughter used to say, “Dad, you would use Tobasco on ice cream!”. I like Cholula much better now because flavor is key.

Delicious. I followed recipe except for the hot sauce. I did not use any during the cooking but added Crystal hot sauce to my plated portion. My wife prefers a lower spice level so she was happy with just the level provided by the black pepper. Thanks Sam Siftion for mentioning this recipe in your 3/21 newsletter.
Bold flavors made sublime by all the delicious dairy!  Really good over polenta spiked with Parmesan.

Worked well for 2. Sauce was fab. Used sriracha, because it’s what we had, but only half the quantity. Didn’t change anything else. Served on garlic & olive oil quinoa, but next time I think I’ll try fettuccine. The sauce has a consistency so much like a good Alfredo.
@Karen L Davis we used orrechiette and it was perfect!
Delicious. Used more than a tbsp of hot sauce because I need a little kick with my food. Fam was confused with the name - it's neither BBQ-y nor Peppery.

I kind of misread how much prep work this would be for the Monday after spring break lol. It's a bit of prep. I have a culinary degree and have cooked professionally, and this took me an hour start to finish. That being said, this is DELICIOUS! Honestly, a TB of Crystal (the hot sauce I use) wasn't even that hot after all that cream and butter gets added in. We even added extra hot sauce at the table. 

This is a keeper. Make as written, even if you're scared of the hot sauce.

@Amy
Crystal Hot Sauce - now and forever!

Unfortunately the NYT seems to think that all subscribers have kitchen prep help on speed dial.  I've just completed the prep work.  Peeling shrimp, prepping shallot and garlic, grinding pepper took well over half an hour alone.  Looking forward to trying it since I have a sibling who lives in P-town part time, and raves about the restaurant.  But have given up hoping for a more realistic approach in terms of prep time (and ingredients.  What is a medium shallot, when chopped?).
old bay is the best sub. don't forget to undercook the shrimp in the first step

This is a really tasty dish and I am glad that I read all the comments as we are not fans of heat. My modifications are:
Used sriracha instead of Tabasco- half the amount 
Added 2 tbsp of BBQ sauce- next time will use 1 tablespoon 
Used half the amount of butter
Half and half instead of heavy cream 
Halved the black pepper 
Skipped the rosemary and thyme and instead used fresh basil to finish the plate. 
Definitely adding this to my repertoire.
Rich, but delish!  I am lactose intolerant so I used Ripple half-and-half instead of heavy cream. Other than that I followed the recipe exactly.  Served with crostini.  We both loved it.
I forgot to add the butter completely and this was still SO GOOD
We made this recipe with about 1 1/2 pounds of shrimp (21-25 tail on), no other changes and served it over thin spaghetti and/or crusty baguette. It’s going in the rotation. Delicious!

This is great stuff, but a couple of suggestions:

1. Rather than sprigs of rosemary and thyme, use ¼ of a teaspoon of each.

2. Rather tan pasta or bread, serve over plain white rice.
This was delicious. An absolute keeper. Spicy and creamy and tangy. Served over polenta for a twist on shrimp and grits.
Halved the Tabasco and used only tsp pepper.  Delicious over brown rice.
Spectacular!
I substituted Cholula for the Tabasco. I have two young children. Cholula is less piquant and has garlic. It was a hit with the kids.
Any thoughts on using full fat coconut milk as a substitute for the heavy cream? We are dairy-free and this looks delish!
See note from Nikki 2 below.  She used cream of coconut.
This was delicious. Definitely going into our rotation. We will do 1.5x or 2x as this was closer to two servings than four for us.
Followed recipe exactly except maybe an extra Tbs or two of cream. Came together quickly once garlic/shallot chopped. Wished I'd browned the shrimp a touch longer. Serves about 2 1/2. Yum!.
question on the thyme and rosemary sprigs.  should you just strip and chop the leaves off the sprigs? Or just throw them in with the wine?
Just throw them in, then pick the stems out later. So much easier! Cheers!

Sorry to ask this if it's already been asked, but can anyone recommend a non-dairy substitute for the heavy cream? Don't get me wrong, I LOVE dairy products but my stomach doesn't agree, so any hints, tips, or cheats would be much appreciated.
Way too much Tabasco. Use half and it should be good.

I am (generally) a terrible and certainly uninventive cook (I even use that term generously ;) ).  I am, however, good at following directions.  This was truly delicious, and my wife and daughter ate large portions!
This was delicious. I don’t think it tastes like BBQ but tastes like scampi with a little spice. It’s so tasty I would try to make this sauce with other meats or mushrooms.

Tobasco-style hot sauce varies considerably by brand in heat and salt level. I would taste the hot sauce to assess heat-level and the label for sodium-level (In the grocery, some time, take a look at sodium levels on the various hot sauces). Correct seasoning at the end, after incorporating the butter (I like salted), by adding more Tabasco if desired, then salt, if needed.
The protein/sauce ratio should vary according to the side. Pasta, normally (that would not be me!) not a lot. Bread, rice, or roast potatoes will want more.

Delicious.  I subbed chipotle tabasco for regular tabasco for a bit of smokiness and reduced the black pepper a bit (spice averse partner), and the sauce was to die for.  Served over linguini and decided the best restaurant is my own kitchen.
Made exactly to recipe and it was a hit! Only thing my husband asked to change is cut the Worcestershire sauce by half.

Delicious - my husband called it "restaurant quality". Made it exactly as suggested. Used jumbo shrimp so the 2 1/2 minutes per side was fine, but if the shrimp were smaller than jumbo, I'd recommend less searing time. The tabasco and pepper did not make it too spicy. Served it with crusty bread.
Put the grilled bread on the bottom and soak it with shrimp and sauce! This is amazing and delicious.

You have to use heavy cream and not half and half or it will separate with any acid, as I learned with a different NYT recipe calling for heavy cream. Next time I’ll know. (That recipe used lemon juice in the sauce, this has wine and not sure what reducing the wine will mean to the cream.)
This was absolutely delicious, but the flavor only came together after adding back the partially cooked shrimp (and those juices). Sauce is so good I could drink it right from the pan!
fabulous shrimp.   not quite pascals manles bbq shrimp, but a delicious variation on that theme.

Sorry, but that amount of Tabasco makes this recipe nearly inedible. First, I would find the original McNulty recipe if you want a really tasty dinner BUT also use you judgement with regards to the amount of ground black pepper  - it calls for 5TBS while 2 might be a better starting point. You will also see that the original recipe calls for only 1/2 tsp of Tabasco compared to 1TBS in the NYT recipe. Both recipes have the same ingredients but the original is a much better balance.
Way too much tabasco which skews the balance of flavors. The original McNulty Lobster Pot recipe is far superior.

I was so disappointed with this recipe. Followed to a T and just hated it. I have enjoyed so many other recipes from NYT and this was just a fail. Texas gal that loves spice and the Tabasco and Wooster sauce amounts were completely overwhelming. Always fun to try something new in the kitchen, but this is a one and done for me. My husband said he liked it, but he was starting to appease my mood LOL
This was fantastic! I was concerned that it called for so much Tabasco and black pepper, but it was perfect. If you like really hot stuff you could add even more!Served it with linguini.

SO delicious! Spicy, creamy, savory, used salted butter and didn’t need to add salt aside from a lite dusting on the shrimp before cooking. Made it exactly as described plus a bit more shrimp and broccoli and broccolini as sides, plus a loaf of rustic Italian bread. Next time if adding more shrimp would also have a bit more sauce, otherwise perfect!
This sauce is BOMB. Used Frank's Red Hot. Delicious perfection

High return for relatively little effort! I did use Greek yogurt instead of cream, red onion instead of shallot because that’s what I had and vermouth was not shy about the Tabasco. It was excellent. I put it with good ravioli.

Delicious. I made it once with just shrimp tails and a second time with whole shrimp (with head). If you are able to, I recommend using whole shrimp. The liquid gold (shrimp brains) just brings the entire dish together, adding a sweet accent to the tangy, creamy, spicy sauce.

I didn't have fresh thyme or rosemary, I used dried and it still tasted fantastic!

I have eaten kilt lettuce all my life but never from old lettuce.  Living in the North Carolina mountains we highly anticipated those first leaves of fresh lettuce in the spring.  We cut up spring onions with the lettuce and killed it with bacon grease or more likely fatback grease but never with store bought iceberg lettuce.  Sometimes we could find wild branch lettuce which worked very well.
@Bonnie Dodson Exactly. I took one look at the picture and could tell it was all wrong.
Kilt lettuce is made from fresh lettuce, straight from the garden.  It is wilted by the hot bacon grease.  Not from old wilted lettuce.
Romaine? Should be leaf lettuce straight from the garden. Wilted lettuce salad - first taste of spring in East Tennessee. Or killed salad, kilt is what bagpipers wear! Anyhow, always a favorite.

My grandmother from Tennessee did not use honey nor old lettuce (yuck).  She used red leaf lettuce and scallions, mixed the greens and bacon together. Then she made sure the bacon grease was almost smoking hot before pouring it over the mix. Then added the vinegar and salt to taste.  She called it wilted lettuce. One of my favorite dishes as a child.
I’ve never made this but have eaten it, and it’s delicious. Some cooks add chopped or sliced hard-boiled eggs to it.

The word killed,or kilt,has nothing to do with the state of the lettuce.  It is made with the first spring greens, or early leaf lettuce, which is “killed” or wilted, by the hot drippings. Iceberg lettuce is a contrivance which never has had, nor will have, a role in this ancient Appalachian dish.

I’ll add my vote to the consensus here that this dish, which my north Louisiana nana called wilted lettuce salad, is made from fresh leaf lettuce, never ever iceberg, fresh or otherwise. Green onions, hot bacon grease, apple cider vinegar and chopped boiled eggs are essential.

I've never heard of kilt lettuce salad being made with old lettuce, Lordy me! It's a spring delicacy made with fresh-picked greens (usually wild and/or leaf lettuces; nobody's growing iceberg lettuce in the mountains). Many generations of my Appalachian Kentucky family relish kilt lettuce as the first fresh salad of spring, with thinly sliced fresh radishes and green onions or wild ramps.
Use cabbage rather than lettuce and you  get Hot German slaw that this Yankee boy grew up eating.
This brings me back to my childhood. Agreed with others though who use fresh lettuce, not wilted, for this dish. My favorite version uses about half the vinegar and no sweetener.

Killed, or kilt, lettuce is generally considered a spring dish, once commonly made with wild branch lettuce, but it can be made with any fresh, tender-crisp salad greens—not lettuce that has been languishing on the counter or in the fridge.
Grew up with kilt lettuce. Used leaf lettuce from the garden. east tennessee classic.

It’s October near the Great Lakes and I had all the ingredients in my kitchen, but the aging iceberg lettuce just looked too pathetic. Outside in my garden I still had some Green Wave mustard greens growing. Bushels of it!  It has more delicate leaves than some other leafy greens, but is still mustard. Raw, it is very spicy, but cooked it is mild and sweet. It vaguely resembles a lettuce, but spicy enough raw that the rabbits won’t eat it.

 I substituted a generous amount of the Green Wave mustard greens and used it instead of the tired iceberg.  I tore the leaves with my hands and included the ribs and stems.  This particular variety of greens cooks fast, and can turn to mush, so I watched carefully.

The well-cooked leaves were like cooked lettuce, the ribs juicy. Some less-cooked leaves still had a mustardy bite, but the rest was sweet, and so good in this recipe!

The bacon, apple cider vinegar and honey just made the cooked greens irresistible!  This will be my go-to recipe now for eating my garden-fresh greens.

This is only a spring dish. You need tender baby lettuces from the garden. And we always make ours with early wild onions or ramps if we can get them. Green onions will do if you can't get either. It needs to be mild. And we don't use honey. Sorghum or just a tad of sugar.

Not just a southern dish. My Nana, then my mother made this all the time as I was growing up in the midwest. I've been making this all my adult life too. Never, never with iceberg lettuce or old lettuce. Always made with fresh greens of all kinds, most often with the first harvest from our gardens but also using spinach, or beet, dandelion, collard or whatever greens were fresh and available. Never old greens, never iceberg.

Wilted lettuce salad—made from the garden. All ingredients matter. Leaf lettuce. Bacon fat.  Vinegar. A pinch of sugar. Green onions. A chopped hard boiled egg Best with crumbled bacon. Hearty. Country fare. 
Variation is soaked salad. Italian origin I believe.

In Florida we use this recipe on garden grown lettuce like black seeded simpson.  It’s good even in summer when the lettuce starts turning a bit bitter.  My grandmother dissolved a heaping spoon of sugar in the vinegar though, not honey.

My grandmother from Missouri used to make wilted lettuce salad using romaine, the dressing bacon, vinegar, brown sugar, basically the same thing in today's recipe.  My dad gorged on it, he loved it, I loved it too. The cooking of the bacon came first, then the vinegar and brown sugar and then the romaine was tossed in until it wilted - yummy.
Pretty darn good.  Used maple bacon :)

This recipe is so similar to my Pennsylvania Dutch nana’s Dutch Mess. Whenever we had leftover mashed potatoes, she’d fry up some bacon and add apple cider  vinegar, scallions, and iceberg lettuce chopped. Then she turn it in to Pattie’s and then fry in bacon grease . We called them potato pancakes Nana Style! Delicious!

I grew up eating this and loving it but my mom called it "Wilted Lettuce Salad." :>
I had forgotten about it until I saw this and am ON it ...

My Dad used to pick leaf lettuce from the garden, fry some bacon and make his version of this salad.
Years ago I did a little research and found that this salad originated in France. Made sense as my Dad's maternal grandfather was born in France and emigrated to America when he was young.
Just for fun, I tried making his version from my memory. Wasn't a hit. Maybe that's why my Mom never made it for the whole family and he always made just enough for himself.
I use fresh lettuce from the garden, green onions and fresh radishes. A splash of Dijon mustard in the dressing adds flavor.

I checked out this recipe, wondering if it was like my Southern mother's wilted lettuce recipe (made with fresh lettuce) and realized it was exactly the same thing. "Kilt" refers to the hot bacon drippings being tossed with it, which wilts the fresh lettuce somewhat (but would be somewhat gross with already wilted, old lettuce).

Oh!  My Mom made "wilted lettuce salad" all of the time in the 60s and 70s.  We loved it, and now my husband does too.  I think that my grandmother learned about this in the early 20th C. when she emigrated to Texas (because I'm pretty sure that they knew nothing about lettuce or fried green tomatoes in Moravia, where she grew up.)

I made this recipe exactly as written (shocking!) - used up some old lettuce from the back of the fridge.  It was delicious and satisfying. Next time I may follow the suggestion to throw in a sliced hard boiled egg.
In Northern Ohio we used endive.  Delish.
followed the recipe to a T and it was so gross lol
I use endives instead of iceberg lettuce, and sugar mixed with white vinegar and warm water instead of apple cider vinegar/honey.

This recipe seems more like the wilted lettuce side dish I grew up with than the more recognizable wilted lettuce salad, where warm bacon vinaigrette is poured over fresh greens (leaf lettuce or spinach), then served immediately. We ate wilted lettuce as a cooked side dish (like fried cabbage or stewed tomatoes). The chopped lettuce is cooked (wilted) for 4-5 minutes in the pan with hot bacon grease/vinegar, so you need a lettuce with some backbone, like iceberg, or you'll just get a mushy mess.

I live in Southern California, so I substituted habaneros for the scotch bonnets and cilantro for the culantro. It is absolutely delicious. As with most hot sauces, wear gloves while handling cut peppers or you will be sorry! The Times should add a note about gloves to the recipe.

Scotch bonnets have the best flavor, but that being said, this recipe does not flatter them.  The recipe as is, with 'seeds intact' is unbearably hot without offering the nice flavor of the chilis.  I tried a second batch, removing the seeds, and the sauce was better.  The best way to use scotch bonnet in a soup is not with this sauce but rather to put 1-3 whole chilis into the pot, without breaking them up.  Remove before eating.  Scotch bonnets can be frozen whole and last for a year or more.

Cleaning up the garden and using what I have. I used 15 red jalapenos (which were quite spicy for jalapenos) and 3 poblanos. Very tasty! It's not just hot; it has a great flavor profile. I will make this again...
Can’t wait to make this with my Armageddon peppers!

I can easily find culantro at Asian markets (sometimes labeled "recao"). But I never see scotch bonnets in NJ and we generally have access to harder-to-find ingredients . Even my local Caribbean market doesn't sell them fresh. I've always substituted habaneros out of necessity. Besides growing them, where have my fellow heat junkies found fresh scotch bonnets? Hesitant to order produce online, I've been burned with rotten deliveries at not so hot prices (puns intended).

@Bree LaFin I get scotch bonnets at the farmer’s market in NYC’s Union Square. I don’t know where in NJ you are, but I’ve also gotten them at the Sunday market in Red Bank, and I suspect you might be able to find them if you have a decent-sized farmer’s market near you.

Very tasty! Ended up deseeding my scotch bonnets, 
and used only around 6 or 7 with this recipe, but otherwise sticker with the recipe. Extremely pleased with the results.
Love it! I grew my own scotch bonnets and scorpions this year, and this was the perfect use for 18+ of them. Yes, wear gloves! It is hot...but that's the point.

mmm yummy yummy
big delicious 

i eat
i get happy
Roll that dough into a roughly rectangular shape & cut the cakes in squares. No messing w/ scraps of dough or overworking the dough. Tastes just as good as a square.

I’m 75 and remember my great grandmother’s tea cakes*** at her home in Mississippi.  I am naturally hazy on the precise ingredients, though I watched them being made many times; the big surprise to me in the NYT recipe is the sour cream.
It is no exaggeration to say that these tea cakes, warm and slightly soft from the oven, were my first “favorite food.”
  *** Note: the tea cakes were made by my great grandmother’s cook, “Willie B,” and it is quite likely that the recipe used came from her.

The story made me want to bake these. The weirdnesses in the recipe (including the seemingly random prep/bake times, surprisingly hot oven, and discrepancy between baking powder in the description and baking soda in the ingredients) make me think I should find someone else's recipe.

Now that I’ve made them, I highly recommend. I swapped in Greek yogurt for SC added more fresh nutmeg to my taste. To get the domed cookies I dropped by 1T scoop and slightly flattened them before baking ( way easier than rolling out) and baked at 350 F for about 18 mins. Terrific!

It’s gotta be 350 F. My search of other tea cake recipes show baking soda with acid like sour cream or buttermilk so I think that is the ticket here. If you are swapping ingredients consider this chemistry.
450 is too high. It got burnt in the 450 F temperature  but it tasted  okay if not great.
cookies burned at 450 and didnt rise as high as in  photo
The article notes that Jocelyn Delk Adams of Grandbaby Cakes adds powdered freeze-dried raspberries, which color the dough pink.

I followed the recipe as written and used a 2” round cookie cutter.  I must have rolled them thinner than called for because I ended up with 60 gorgeous little cakes.  They baked in only 8 minutes.  I completed the cakes in 90 minutes; some of that time was spent getting used to rolling dough and cutting the cakes (lots of dusting flour is the key).  Delicious with coffee and I imagine tea, but a bit bland otherwise.  I’ll make these again.
Should I use baking powder or baking soda?  And what is the correct bake time?
I imagine that with the sour cream, baking soda is the correct riser.  That combo, baking soda + something acidic, makes any type of biscuit, cake or pancake very tender and light.

I just made these. After reading the other comments, I baked them at 375º for 12 minutes, and they were perfectly done. However, they were pretty bland. I think this may be one of those recipes that speak to memory as much as taste. I made a chocolate glaze and dipped the tops in, because white cake with chocolate frosting was a childhood favorite, and I liked them much better.

I believe it would be more like 5 minutes to gather and measure all the ingredients prior to mixing, beating and rolling! 
BTW, I really wish most of the recipes out there were more realistic regarding prep time. Not specifically faulting this paper as I find most of published recipes seem to be exceedingly short on prep time. Best to read the through the directions a couple of times prior to starting.
Should the temperature be 350 degrees? Which most cookies are baked at.
Please reduce oven temperature or many will have burnt cookies as other stated.

I have no idea how this recipe makes 18 cakes - I got 3 dozen and, no, I was not rolling the dough to 1/3 the listed 1/2" thickness (I used a ruler).    Otherwise, they were quick to pull together with ingredients on hand.  If I make again I think I'll add lemon zest.
Jeez, factor of 2, not 3...    I rolled to 1/2", not 1/4".
450 degrees was much too hot.  My first batch burned.  I cooked the following batches for eight minutes.  Unfortunately, I found the tea cakes to be dry and bland.  I would not recommend making them.

My great auntie made these for us in little molded muffin tins.  In our favorite version she added cocoa to the batter for chocolate tea cakes.  If there is any food that fills me with nostalgia, it is tea cakes.

450 F seems unlikely
change to "open mouth of a 4-ounce mason/canning jar'?

Followed the recipe since I’m not an expert baker .
Big disappointment ! Unpalatable 
First batch cooked had a very dark brownish color underneath , so I lowered the temperature for the second batch.The cookie did not look, height wise , as in the picture .Those « cookies » are tasteless. The cookies taste NOTHING!unlike in the description , this doesn’t even remotely come close to scones.
I will try to salvage them with some icing/glaze.

Instead of rolling and cutting them out (something I avoid whenever possible), I used a one-ounce disher, resulting in 33 cookies. Based on all the notes below I baked them at 375 for 11 minutes, and they came out well. They have a mild but lovely flavor from the vanilla and nutmeg, and I bet they will be fab with a mug of cocoa.
Threw them out. Burnt on bottom and tasted bland. Should have read the comments before trying instead of relying on the recipe creator.

As another reader pointed out, something's off with this recipe: Prep time given as 5 minutes when beating the butter and sugar alone calls for 5 minutes?
Cook time given as 55 mins when the instructions say to bake for 12 mins? 
A 450 degree oven? 
On a different note, I just started reading Nora Neale Hurston's book, Their Eye's Were Watching God, a classic of the Harlem Renaissance. Wondered about the name "Tea Cake" for one of the male characters. Thanks for the historical background!

As a kid, my grandmother would always have tea cakes on the table when we visited (North Florida).  It was a treat! I do not know all of her ingredients and I am sure there are multiple versions. There were two different ingredients for her tea cakes. She did not use sour cream, rather she used sour milk (after milking the cow) and cane syrup for sweetener. As you might imagine, the syrup yielded a rather robust flavor.
Lower temperature to 400º or 350º?  Perhaps use a slightly larger cutter.
Okay. Not worth the effort.

My daughter wanted to make these, and they were better than I expected. Didn’t look like cakes — looked like biscuits. The nutmeg gave it an interesting flavor, and we used Siggis vanilla yogurt instead of sour cream, which I assume sweetened it up. Would make an interesting base for a strawberry shortcake situation. Also did 350 degree oven based on comments. My 5 year old enjoyed!

stats.Total Time: 1 hour 10 minutes, plus cooling and 2 hours’ chilling
stats.Cook Time: 1 hour, plus cooling and 2 hours’ chilling

comments.commentBody: I would bet that like any jam, you could microwave the tamarind paste for thirty seconds or so and it would be perfectly spreadable.

There's Tamarind Pulp (sold in hard blocks, found in most South Asian, Southeast Asian, and some Latine grocers), Tamarind Concentrate (watery like juice), and the Tamarind Paste called for here, which has a jam-like consistency. You can easily make the paste from the Tamarind Pulp (which might be easier to find) by soaking a chunk of the pulp in hot water, then straining. Hot Thai Kitchen has a great overview of all this: https://hot-thai-kitchen.com/making-tamarind-paste/

For those dealing with gluten issues -  I use Bob's Redhill, King Arthur, Cup for Cup, or Better Batter gluten free flours. Use a measure of 130 grams for a cup.  The real trick is allowing the batter to rest fir 30 minutes before baking. No one can guess my baked goods are gluten free. I learned these tips from Chef Alina on FB.  Good luck.
You want the milk to curdle. It is a vegan substitute for buttermilk.

I made this for my husband’s birthday and it was a big hit. I made as written except I didn’t have the whole spices so I used the ground spices I had on hand in same amounts. I was also worried about using 2tablespoons of baking powder I thought it might be an error so I used 1 tablespoon. The cake rose fine, was moist and delicious. The tamarind’s tartness played beautifully with the sweet frosting. I also added a squeeze of lemon juice to the frosting. This recipe is a keeper!

I made this cake with dairy and it was fantastic.
I used buttermilk and butter
The tamarind paste I found at my local store was very thin and liquidy. I was concerned it wouldn’t make a good filling but I spread it on my cake layers and it combined with the cake to make a delicious middle layer.
I also used a bit of the tamarind paste in the icing. The icing is quite sweet and the tart tamarind helped. 
My family all suggested a cream cheese frosting instead. 
I’m making this again !!

This cake sounds delicious and I would love to make it but I am wondering the 1/2 cup of tamarind paste gets added to the batter-- at the same time as the oil, applesauce and soy milk mixture? Also, if you don't need to make the cake vegan, can you substitute buttermilk for the soy milk and vinegar mixture?
See Step 7.  However, I have never found a tamarind paste that is spreadable on its own.

The tamarind paste is spread between the layers right before frosting (see step 7). I missed it the first time reading through the recipe, too. And I would imagine buttermilk would work as a substitute for the vinegar & soy milk.

Would oat milk work as well as soy milk?

Thank you for posting a cake recipe that does not use eggs -- much appreciated!
Kalustyan's sell Calabash Nutmeg. shop local!( if you are in NYC-or online at: foodsofnations.com)

Just made this for Thanksgiving and it was excellent!  I used 1 1/2 teaspoons regular nutmeg in place of the calabash nutmeg.  I will definitely be making this again but next time will increase the cayenne and add even more tamarind between the layers.
How would I substitute the soy milk for buttermilk? Could I remove the soy milk and apple cider vinegar and sub 1:1 with buttermilk?
For those of us who don't want to go the whole nine yards and grind the spices, how much regular ground nutmeg, cloves, and cardamom?
I have the same question!

I subscribe to NYT Cooking to learn about re spices, ingredients, and techniques. This recipe hit all the boxes, and is one of the most unusual they print. Not being Vegan, Gluten-Free, or willing to buy new versions of things I have, I used only grated nutmeg, regular cardamom, and whole milk for the cake. Loved not needing egg for texture. It’s a huge amount of batter.
   The Vegan Frosting was worth the investment in cashew butter. It really takes some time, but a delightful result!

Made, by request, for my 10 year old's birthday. I reduced the cayenne to a heaping 1/4 t, and was nervous about the 2T of baking powder, so I used about 1 1/2 T. I would use the full amount of baking powder next time. I also reduced the sugar to 200 g which was good. The batter looked very oily in the pan, but turned out great. The tamarind "puree" I found was very thin and not spreadable. We added it to the cream cheese frosting - we're not fans of buttercream - and it was delicious.
Can I replace the soy milk with whole cow’s milk, and if so, any issue with curdling with the apple cider?
These are the recipes that make me miss working with gluten, but I’m gonna try it with a gf mix because I can’t pass up tamarind in a spice cake. That’ll be my only substitute. Here goes
Let us know how it goes! I too need to often try GF subs in baking.
What is “vegan butter” and would real milk work in lieu of the soy?
There are a few excellent brands of vegan butter that substitute perfectly, in my experience, for dairy butter. Miyoko's is my favorite, and Trader Joe's vegan butter is also good.

Nutritional information states a serving of this cake has 200 carbs.
Two hundred carbohydrates.

(I'll regain consciousness momentarily)
Either vinegar or lemon juice will curdle vegan milk just like cow’s milk.
1/2 teaspoon of cayenne sounds like alot.

We prefer desserts that are less sweet-tasting and use less sugar. Used Ermine Frosting, which uses less sugar and has a slightly nutty taste. Paired with the tangy element of tamarind, the cake was a hit for a family birthday. 

Made it gluten-free, no issues.

So great flavor, and I followed recipe closely, but whoa, amount of liquid and cook time for the rice way off. Did the 20 min heat + 15 off heat (undisturbed) and rice was crunchy. Another 0.5 cup water, 5 min, twice, then getting frustrated so put 1 cup water for 5 more minutes. Then ANOTHER cup and another 5 minutes and turned up the heat. Disappointing results—no fluffy, all mushy 😑.

@Ilsa K. I hate to suggest this…but is there a chance that you didn’t put a lid on the pot? A tight one, which would’ve kept all the water from boiling away?

Your saga (which is definitely disheartening!) sounds like exactly what would happen if a person was boiling rice in an uncovered pot.
thats what I was thinking, or too high of heat. I fillowed exactly and it was the perfect amount of liquid and time.@Maria in Minneapolis
I think recipe should specify the amount of water to add to chicken bouillon and boil, prior to step 3. Sounds good and very simple. Nice.

The Tomato Mixture (which includes 2 tomatoes, a large bell pepper, and 3 chilis pureed together) added immediately after the bouillon would presumably be enough liquid to dissolve the 1 tablespoon of bouillon without additional water or a pre-boiling step - that's why bouillon "granules" are specified (not cubes).
what amount of water does the recipe suggest? two tomatoes do not seem to be sufficient amount of liquid for 2 1/4 cups of thirsty (basmati) rice.
The tomato mixture added in Step 4 includes 2 C water. In Step 3 you only stir to coat the rice, then add the liquid in Step 4.

*Use 3c. of water*
As written, there is not enough water in this recipe. I relied on my go-to rice cooking method: 1. Rinse rice until water runs clear
2. Use 1 1/3c. water for every cup of rice.  I cut the recipe by about half: 1 1/2 c. rice, so kept 2 c. water in step 1. 
3. Bring to a boil before reducing to low simmer and covering. Cooked as directed (20mins on, 15mins off). 
The dish was perfect. Will be making every week!
Never heard that West Africans use olive oil! West African cuisine typically relies more on locally available oils like palm oil, groundnut (peanut) oil, and coconut oil.
The flavor of this rice dish was excellent. Only used one Serrano chili and it was way too spicy. Recipe is correct is saying enough for a crowd. I’d make again with another adjustment to the heat.
i read the recipe several times but could not figure out the amount of liquid -- soup, water? which is key to a good rice dish
The tomato mixture prepped in Step 1, then added to the rice in step 4, includes 2 cups of water (in addition to pureed tomatoes etc.)
I personally use more water than this, but one option is to soak the basmati rice first (as is done in a lot of S Asian cooking), and then drain it. It will need less water.

The two cups of water should be included in the tomato mixture part of the recipe. Otherwise it's a great recipe, make sure to stir the rice instead of letting it sit, as the recipe says. Because I ended up with half crunchy half done rice.

I have a much simpler jollof rice recipe that does not require blending the tomato mixture. Saute the onion as in step 2, then add the tomato mixture ingredients without blending first, then rice. If rice isn't completely cooked in 35 minutes (I use brown rice that takes longer), simply cover it for additional time after making sure the base mixture is boiling before adding the rice, as the recipe recommends. I leave the pot on the burner so residual heat aids the cooking.
If you can find it at a Mediterranean market, try it with SMOKED basmati rice!
Re the (missing?)amount of water: some say it’s mentioned in either step 2 or step 4. I do not see any additional liquid mentioned anywhere. Please advise. TIA.
Yes, there's 2 c. of water in the tomato mixture, but even with the other veg, that does not seem like enough water for over 2 c. of rice. Is the chicken bouillon supposed to be reconstituted?
I found that the rice was not fully cooked and started burning.  Maybe needs more water.

VERY flavorful. I omitted the cayenne pepper since there already are two whole serranos and one whole habanero in the recipe, and the jollof is still pleasantly hot. 
I also heeded the suggestion of a commenter and added more water than specified in the recipe, but I went too far in the other direction and ended up with very moist rice. Next time, I’ll go with the 2 cups and see where I end up. 
Finally, 20 minutes on medium-low resulted in something like a tahdig on the bottom of the dish. If you like that, no problem. If you want uniformly cooked rice, turn it down to low.

Rice started to burn… .  Seems like totally insufficient liquid.  Rescued it into another pot and added water.  Next time I will pre soak the rice or try even lower heat.  When using a cast iron pot, the temperature stays high for longer, perhaps that explains the difference?
@Hans Deseure same thing happened to me. Even after switching pots the rice was still catching on the bottom of the pan

I wouldn’t make this recipe again. The rice burnt twice as there is not enough liquid in the recipe. I am a confident cook and managed to save it but the rice did not cook uniformly through the recipe without stirring it. I halved the chilli but it was still nice and spicy. Good flavours despite having to babysit the cooking process.

Perfection! Very straightforward preparation, yielding very flavorful results. This is a recipe I will save and make many times over. I cooked this in an enameled cast iron pan, and stirred twice during cooking only because I am terrible at judging the simmering temp for rice. It still came out great. I also subbed unrefined palm oil for half of the olive oil. I highly recommend trying that, for a little smoky funk.
I would cut back the amount of oil used and also think 20 minutes wasn't enough cooking time - rice came out a bit toothy. I also subbed out Poks Jollof Rice seasoning for all the spices - delicious!

I followed the recipe amounts exactly, and used one of my wide le creuset pots with a lid and I also had crunchy rice after 35 minutes. I added more water and gave another 5 minutes twice, still crunchy. Then did more water and about 6 more minutes. It still probably could have used a little more time but we were hungry.

This is.... ok. I went with a 14oz can of whole tomatoes because off-season tomatoes have no flavor, and I figured it could use the extra liquid anyway. Then I filled the can and added that amount of water to the blender. It's strange because the dish is still moist, the rice didn't burn, but it also didn't really cook? I soaked the rice for about 20 minutes but maybe I should have soaked it longer. Don't usually have this issue with basmati rice. The rice should be parboiled, honestly.

Even after reading the comments and adjusting my prep, this rice took several rounds of adding water and more cook time . The flavor was good though I seeded all my peppers to reduce the heat. I used brown rice , pre soaked it for 30 minutes and started with 1:1.75 rice to water ratio. Ultimately it needed at least 45 minutes and at least 1:2 cups of water per cup of rice.

This was really really good on day one. On day two the addition of large shrimp made it sing (and me, too). Using 2c of water, I followed the recipe exactly with the exception of using two large heirloom tomatoes and Better Than Bouillon rather than granules. Used a large round le Cruset baking pan (which ended up completely full) and was pleasantly surprised that the rice was cooked perfectly using the specified times. I’m glad it made lots!
Just remade this and used parboiled rice instead. Came out perfectly cooked, tender without being mushy. You simply cannot use basmati for jollof rice.

title: Saladu Nebbe (Black-Eyed Pea Salad With Tomatoes and Cucumbers) Recipe
author: Korsha Wilson

comments.commentBody: When I prepare this again I will reverse the olive oil and lime juice measurements to 1/4 cup olive oil and 1/2 cup lime juice. Olive oil at 1/2 cup thickly coats everything and pools in the bottom of the bowl and lime juice is nearly imperceptible at the original measures.

@Bill S.

I was thinking same as I read the recipe, or maybe 1:1 oil/acid.
You could also start with boiling dried black-eyed peas, since black-eyed peas cook really fast (no soaking required). A good way to control the amount of salt.

Given food restrictions, omitted lime juice and chiles but with a very light EVOO this was great with the other ingredients, a bit of juice from a few tomatoes and diced raw zucchini instead of red peppers (also restricted). For us, no need for s/p. Next time might throw in a second herb and red onion and would then let it sit longer. A great use of b.e.p., home cooked or canned, as a light supper with cornbread, corn or the cob or, to mix things up, grilled pita or a traditional flatbread.
@Susan C. Instead of adding another herb, what about mixing in cut-up kale  or spinach or other light salad greens?

Made as written with fresh ingredients, only exception was using black eyed peas from dried. Salad was soupy, oily and bland. I removed over 1/2 cup of liquid & I should've removed more. If I make again, I will use dressing ratio like Samin Nosrat's Salad-e Shirazi (or just add black eyed peas to that recipe). People did eat it at the potluck but def not a crowd pleaser.

well, at least that way you got lots of “test tasters” and feedback.

I am reminded of my mother, who told me not to make a dish for the first time for guests — and presumably to share at potlucks. But, as the name says, its’ a “pot-luck.”

@Deb V. 
How could it possibly have been soupy? Did you not drain your beans after cooking?
What liquid were you removing?
If it was bland, that suggests you undersalted your beans when you cooked them.

Made as written except for starting with dried black eyed peas and cooking them in an Instant Pot (if anyone needs it, stir one cup black eyed peas with three cups water or broth in the insert, then cook on high pressure for nine minutes, then natural release for fifteen minutes). Very bright flavor and great as a side with grilled tofu.

Thanks so much for the recipe!  I ate this salad often in Brazil, could never get it to taste quite right once back in Canada. Hadn’t realized it was originally a West African dish, but that totally makes sense, so much Brazilian cooking is based on or adapted from what enslaved people there made.
Le the peas sit in the marinade all night, and finish the salad the next day. Be sure to add garlic to the marinade.
I make this with dried black eyed peas and love it so much. Sometimes I add in extra herbs if I have them in the kitchen and I blitz them into the salad dressing with an immersion blender.

To all those complaining of blandness, as with all cooking, you can adjust the seasoning with salt, lime juice, herbs, and chile to to taste. If you find this dish bland, you are under seasoning it, period.

Swapped the amount of oil and lime juice (1/2 cup lime juice and 1/4 cup olive oil). I seasoned the vinaigrette before adding any of the ingredients with 2 cloves of minced garlic, 1/4 tsp of grated fresh ginger, salt and black pepper. Make sure you like the flavor of the vinaigrette before adding the rest of the ingredients. Also important to emulsify the vinaigrette with a good whisking so it won’t separate and pool oil at the bottom of the bowl. I de-seeded the cucumbers hoping to introduce less water and left the salad to marinate for a few hours in the fridge, mixing it every now and then. Used flat leaf parsley, 2 jalapeños, canned black eyed peas (which significantly decreases the labor involved and ensures the beans are firm). Lots of prep time but it’s delicious and nutritious. Make it your own!
I found this a bit bland.  Might need to add some additional condiments to up the flavor.

@Megan
Try adding more seasoning
Use less oil and more lime
This was a disappointing recipe for me. I made it as directed using fresh tomatoes and peppers from our garden and it turned out bland. I feel like it’s missing some key ingredient/spices.
I used fresh black eyed peas, cut the olive oil in half, and substituted jalapeños since I didn't have serranos... soooo good!
This is really good! My husband and I loved it, kids thought it was fine, but it’s a hit at potluck dinners. It reminds me of tabouli!

If you google the recipe the ingredients are almost always the same.  I would say it is a healthy salad that makes you feel well after eating it, but simple in flavor.  I added garlic, cumin, smoked paprika, cilantro, red onions and red wine vinegar, and omitted the tomatoes and cukes, but the flavors remained both complex.  I added salt and pepper and oil and lime and vinegar at the plate to keep the salad fresh for leftovers.  Would make again because it really does feel healthy, not everything we eat makes you feel that way 😉
So you didn't really make this recipe with all the substitutions.
Drain both peas and veg well. I salted the chopped veg separately in a strainer and got a half cup liquid out. Don’t like soupy salad.

Just made it as written. Very good. Next time I will cook the dried BEPs instead of using canned. My canned ones were a little too firm for my liking. I felt they were slightly undercooked. I didn’t let the salad sit before trying because I was too hungry. I heated up some leftover white rice and put a couple of spoonfuls of the salad on top. A fabulous and filling lunch,
Holy smokes!   So much more than the sum of its parts.  I served it to rave reviews and it held for a couple days worth of Can’t-Wait-For-It lunches.

I used Pinto and Turtle Beans.  Like some of the other commentors I felt the dish as very bland.  There was really no seasoning taste at all.  Ive been experimenting with ingredients to add flavour.  I addded 1/2 teaspoon each of cumin and chile powder.  That was not enough, so I added fresh mint and basil.  That seemed to do the trick - I don't have any but I think fresh cilantro would be a good addition.  I would reconsider the curly parsley

Postive--good mix of vegetables, very healthy, great pick for farm season in NJ.  Negative--bland!, and a little watery.  (I seeded the cucumber, but not the tomatoes.) When making the dressing, definitely add your favorite spices for pizzazz.  (For me, cumin and a pinch of cayenne.)  And it seems a little fussy to have scallions AND shallots, and neither added enough flavor.  I will use red onions next time.  Also, I think I will sub cilantro for the parsley to see if that helps.

I used Glory canned black eyed peas, which are seasoned and worked well. Since they’re salted, no further salt was needed, as well.  But for my tastes, the amounts of scallions and the shallot were excessive.  I’d reduce the scallions in particular by half, or more. It also might help to de water the cucumber, but of course that would increase the saltiness. On the whole, a very nice summer salad that works well with sliced ham.
This salad is excellent without the oil.
Thank you, thank you, thank you!!! We try to avoid added oil, so I am thrilled to see your comment. Did you make any other changes, beyond leaving out the oil?

@RenoRose
Try lengthening time spent emulsifying olive oil, lime juice and parsley (first step). I’ve made this recipe twice and ended up with sore arm after step one but no olive oil collected at the bottom of the bowl.  I can’t imagine leaving out the oil!

I made as directed but used Italian flat leaf parsley because I prefer it.  I liked it, but agree it needs a bit more of a punch…I don’t think the lime juice quite carries the acidity. I added a few splashes of red wine vinegar. Easy and so nutritious. Parsley is a superfood, so was happy to find this recipe.

My mother made all kinds of bean salads during the summer. For her dressing it was always 3 things : 1 part oil (EVO usually), more than 1 part vinegar, less than 1 part sugar or honey, clove or two crushed garlic, and s/p...then she'd put that in a small sauce pan and bring to a simmer to dissolve the sugar, she'd let it cool for a few min, then adjust s/p or sweetness. Boiling helps marry all the ingredients, you never have a salad that seems oily or acidic. Really marinates the beans.
Sounds good but I much prefer the Gullah Geeche version from SoCarolina! Pretty much the same except no Serranos -- red pepper instead and has a light amount of cooked rice! Yum ...

“Sounds good but I much prefer…”

Did you try this one?
I I have been making a version of this for years. Except I use fresh salsa from the dairy case at the store as well as lots of lime juice and no oil at all.
Used cannellini beans and Megan’s Aleppo seasoning instead of jalapeño pepper

I did as one commenter suggested and swapped the lime juice/olive oil amounts. Still, my husband and I found this dish to be totally 'meh.' No flavor whatsoever. I added crumbled feta in an attempt to give it any kind of flavor at all, but ultimately the entire salad wound up in the trash. It's especially disappointing given the amount of time required slicing and dicing.

Made for a potluck picnic. I used cilantro because it was in my CSA share and I needed to use it up,  macho chiles and lemon (instead of Serranos and lime because that's what I had). I also added a couple of dashes of Aleppo pepper. Next time I would add the cucumbers and tomatoes just before serving, or drain them for a while before adding, and maybe some cumin. But I'll make again.

It seems that salads are very forgiving!

Thanks for these suggestions.
Made as written and it turned well, but next time I'll use a lot less (or no) oil and add some other herbs, maybe basil, and garlic or cumin.
I added extra black pepper, minced garlic and cumin. I also tossed 3-4 times over 2 hours,
Easy, excellent, and holds up well in the fridge! The lime adds a nice bright flavour. I halved the olive oil and didn’t miss it.
I am wondering if lime juice is too mild for a vegetable salad. It works so well in fruit salad but I had to add red wine vinegar and lemon juice and lemon zest to pep it up.

The beauty of this recipe is the simplicity of the ingredients. It is the perfect side for heavy flavored entrees. We had it with buttermilk marinated chicken legs that were cooked on our kamado grill with pecan wood. You have to like chopping veggies for this one.
My sister has been bringing this salad to family events for years.  She calls it Red-neck Caviar.
Used drained black eyed peas from dried.  Used 1/3 cup olive oil.  It’s delicious.

Halved the recipe and cut olive oil and lime juice to 3TB each and thought it was perfect. I bought a Serrano for this but it’s lost in my fridge so used a few shakes of black & red pepper blend and it had a nice kick. Looking forward to remaking, with Serrano, to get that herbaceous vibe. Was a great side for oven-fried catfish.
If you have a garden full of other types of tomatoes like I do, you can use them in this recipe.  But, you should sweat them first.

title: Shrimp Dumplings With Saffron Shallot Sauce Recipe
author: Korsha Wilson

comments.commentBody: Sounds delicious and I can't wait to try it.  But I have to comment that crushing the saffron threads in a paper towel strikes me as a very bad idea.   A significant amount of the saffron powder would become caught in the textured paper towel and lost.  As one of the most expensive spices, that seems like an unnecessary waste.  I would use parchment paper.  Even a clean sheet of printer paper likely would be preferable to a paper towel.

Made this and followed the recipe as written.

The saffron sauce is delightful. Worth it to find some higher quality saffron to have that lovely flavor come through the entire dish - Penzey’s Afghanistan saffron was perfect.

For two people, the recipe makes plenty of dumplings/filling. Make sure to dice the shrimp up as fine as you can (almost to a paste), so that when it cooks and plumps a bit, it’s still easy to manipulate into the dumpling wrappers.
I've been to Lapis while visiting Washington--a great spot!  Where does one source the wrappers?  --could wonton wrappers be substituted?  or are Afghan dumpling wrappers something else again?
Use a mortar and pestle for the saffron.

This was phenomenal! My only note is that my sauce did not come out the same color as the picture; mine looked more like the color of the dumpling wrappers. It was still delicious but not very beautiful. Next time, I might try to bloom the saffron first in hot water or add some turmeric to the sauce to give it more vibrancy.
By far, the most delicious thing I have made off of New York Times cooking. My guests were incredulous on how flavorful, nuanced and decadent this dish was. Worth the effort.
I was underwhelmed with this recipe. The sauce was too heavy for the shrimp dumplings. It seemed too rich and fatty overall.
I’d love to also have your recipe for the handmade dumpling wrappers! I’m looking forward to making this dumpling recipe. Thank you!
I order this dish whenever I have lunch at Lapis.  I'm anxious to try making them myself.

Very yummy and were a hit. I made half with shrimp and half with pork sausage cause my wife doesn't eat shrimp. Still worked well in my opinion. If your family isn't big on sauces consider halving the sauce quantity. I have sooooo much leftover.
can this be frozen?
A coffee grinder works well for crushing saffron, as does a mortar and pestle.
I don’t see why you couldn’t dissolve the saffron in the wine, instead of crushing it.
I’ve frozen the dumplings. Can I freeze the sauce?
I’ve made the dumplings and froze them. Can I freeze the sauce?
are these Shanghai or Hong Kong wrappers?

This is a wonderful recipe! I put them in a steamer basket rather than boil them to preserve the flavor. I'd also suggest to not overcook the shrimp (maybe two minutes). They are going to finish cooking and meld with the other ingredients while steaming.

I have been crabbing and eating crabs in South Carolina for 75 years. The Old Bay seasoning in this recipe will overwhelm the sweet, delicate flavor of the crab and you might as well be eating cod. Our quick method to prepare crab was to soak white bread with a little milk, butter, eggs, and mayonnaise, add seasoning to taste and stir in the crab.
There's only 1 t. of Old Bay mixed with a pound of crabmeat, not excessive but you could always start with a smaller amount and add more if desired.

On the west coast,  this can be made with rock crabs. Several species abound and one can catch them off a pier or jetty w/out a license.  All you need is a crab net, some bait, and a free afternoon.  They're larger than blues,  way smaller than Dungeness, and delicious.
My grandmother would put breadcrumbs and melted butter on top then put in the over and broiler.  Have to use real crab backs though,  so much better !

Tampa  has it's own deviled crab  specialty.  The ingredients look basically the same but in Tampa they are made into a croquette and deep fried.  It is not something you make at home but Cuban restaurants serve them and it is very tempting to add one to your order, splash it with hot sauce and eat it while your meal is being prepared.
They’re so much better when you catch them yourself!
Where's the coconut sour cream recipe to accompany?
Is there any reason this wouldn't work (in favor of something quicker) to chop up the cauliflower, then roast it with the sauce?

Outstanding. I quartered it to serve like a lettuce wedge and drizzled pan drippings over it. Also, I had a bit of glaze leftover and brought it to the table. The whole shebang disappeared. It's definitely a party piece that guests will rave about.

Made with jalepeno since that’s what I had. Used the whole habanero, seeded. Pretty good spice level. Starts as very mild, seems too little. By the time I finished a slice, it all came together perfectly. Will make again.

You can simplify and expedite this recipe by using the excellent Walkerswood jerk paste, available in mild and spicy. I used to make my own, but this is as good or better, and is now my go-to. https://walkerswood.com/
@Jah Neekay do you add anything to it to make it “glaze-y” or just brush on the paste and call it a day?
@Jess Add some sugar or maple syrup.

I cook the whole cauliflower head in our Big Green Egg smoker and finish it off under the broiler in the kitchen.  The glaze, enhanced by the smokiness from the grill, is absolutely delicious.  It's an easy summer grill recipe for us.  If there are leftovers, I add some chicken stock and turn it into cauliflower soup for the next day.

Two suggestions. First, just buy Walkerswood jerk seasoning. It’s as good as anything I’ve had in Jamaica or made myself. You can add sugar to it and thin it out with water to make it a glaze. Second, if you put this under the broiler, only the very top of the cauliflower will get blackened. For the second step, I would just raise the oven temperature to 425-450 and roast it until it’s browned.
Would the recipe leave extra glaze either unused (or in the skillet after basting) that could be drizzled on at the table?

This was the first NYT recipe that simply did not work well for me. A total flop. Most importantly, though...one tablespoon of salt has to be a mistake. I started adding a tablespoon and then stopped halfway. It was ridiculously salty...and I'm a salt lover.

This was DELICIOUS. My family doesn’t like spicy foods, so used plain sunflower oil instead of Chile oil, added a couple teaspoons of gochugaru instead of fresh spicy pepper, but otherwise added all the ingredients. Served with coconut rice, and the residual sauce was delicious on that as well! Will definitely make again.
Can anyone provide a recipe for the coconut sour cream that's recommended as an accompaniment please?
I’ve seen it in stores. Could probably also use coconut yogurt

My broiler is under the oven and isn't nearly high enough to stand up a head of cauliflower.  Suggestions?  I could just put it back in the oven with the glaze.  Or maybe cut it in half top to bottom and lay each half down which I think would fit in the broiler space.
Halfing it would work. The recipe calls for it whole as it's quite a dramatic presentation however, the flavor won't change with halfing it.

Ick! I love Gregory Gourdet, and ET is my fav cookbook of all time--it's refreshingly modern and comforting. This is the ONLY recipe that was a complete failure in the whole book. Burned, unevenly seasoned, and somehow still waterlogged after ALL the time and broiling.
Where’s the coconut sour cream?  I made it and now can’t find it. This is an amazing dish

Definitely the best whole head roasting process I've tried and will be replicating with other seasonings; sauce was very flavorful and plenty spicy without a Scotch bonnet or habanero but it makes so much in volume I have to think of other ways to use it up
Delicious, love the spice.  After it was cooked, so easy to tear apart and serve as a side.
Trader Joe’s has coconut amino.

I didn't want to heat up the whole house, so I cooked the head of cauliflower in the slow cooker, then broiled it with the sauce in the toaster oven.  Still delicious.  I think an air fryer would also work well with the florets broken up.

title: Gumbo Z’Herbes With Crab and Prawns Recipe
author: Korsha Wilson

comments.commentBody: If you get shrimps with their heads and shells on, and don’t want the heads on the shrimp when serving, you can quickly simmer up a shrimp stock for the liquid in this. Simply bring a pot of water to boil, adding a sliced onion and the tops and bottoms of a few stalks celery.   Also in the water a teaspoon peppercorns and a few bay leaves.  While this is at a good simmer peel your shrimp, and place the heads and shells in the simmering liquid and let it go another 15-20 minutes before straining.

I don’t under stand why this is so good. It is perfect for halibut.  Makes a lot, 1.5 gallons or so.  May not keep long.  Using frozen vegetables it is a pantry dish.

Jalapeños = 1 4 oz can green Chile+ some leftover Thai red chili in for 5 min
Shrimp= 1pound frozen halibut + 1/2 pound frozen red salmon in 2 inch chunks
Greens= 10 oz frozen spinach+10 oz frozen kale
Okra= 4 oz frozen chopped green beans
Stock = 2 Tbsp instant veggie boullion
